ci: Stories are shared in different ways.
lo: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

s.d: Students step into the role of storytellers by recording themselves acting out scenes from familiar or original stories using the green screen. They select digital backgrounds that match their narrative settings, bringing their imaginative tales to life and sharing them with the class in a vibrant, multimedia showcase.

Students animate their own retellings of favourite stories using Stop Motion Studio, bringing characters and settings to life frame by frame. Working in small groups, they use classroom props and simple backgrounds to visually sequence events, reinforcing narrative structure and creative expression in a playful, hands-on way.

Students collaboratively build a digital class storybook using Book Creator on iPads. Each child contributes a page with their own drawing, voice recording, and simple text, showcasing the many ways stories can be imagined and shared with an audience.

Students document their story-creation process by capturing photos and short videos of their developmental play (using costumes, puppets, Lego, etc.) and uploading them to Seesaw. They can add voice reflections about their stories, allowing families to see and hear the diversity of storytelling in the classroom.

Students use PicCollage on iPads to visually sequence the beginning, middle, and end of a familiar or original story using photos of their play, drawings, or props. This supports understanding of story structure and provides a visual, tactile way to share stories with peers.

Students work in small groups to design and build a large, tactile 'adventure map' for a familiar or original story using cardboard, fabric scraps, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each group creates 3D settings (forests, houses, rivers, caves) and props that represent key story locations or events. They then program a Bee-Bot or Sphero Indi to travel across the map, stopping at each location to trigger a storytelling moment?students use puppets, costumes, or their own voices to narrate or act out that part of the story. This project combines hands-on building, collaborative storytelling, and basic robotics, allowing students to express stories physically and digitally while practicing sequencing and creative problem-solving through the Design Cycle.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Stories are shared in different ways' through imaginative play, daily storytelling, retelling familiar stories, and hands-on activities using props, costumes, puppets, and digital tools. The unit emphasizes student agency, creativity, and multimodal expression, including a Mystery Storyteller program, narrative writing, and an Indigenous storytelling excursion.

ci: Everyone has a role to play in the community.
lo: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

s.d: Learners create digital puppet shows in Puppet Pals, role-playing different community helpers and school staff to demonstrate their understanding of roles and responsibilities. Students record their voices and animate characters, sharing their collaborative stories with the class to celebrate the diversity of their school community.

Students take photos of staff members or community helpers (with permission) or draw their own, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short voice messages about each person's role in the community, fostering understanding of diverse contributions.

Collaboratively build a digital class book in Book Creator titled 'Our Community Heroes', where each student adds a page with a drawing, photo, or audio recording about a different community member's job and why it is important.

Design a large floor map of the school community and have students program Bee-Bots to travel between different 'community roles' stations (e.g., classroom, office, library), narrating the journey and discussing the importance of each stop.

Guide students to create vibrant visual collages in PicCollage representing the different roles and connections in their school community, using photos, stickers, and drawings. These can be printed or shared digitally to celebrate community diversity.

Students work in small groups to select a school community role (e.g., librarian, gardener, crossing guard). Using recycled materials, craft supplies, and simple mechanisms (wheels, levers), each group builds a physical model of their chosen helper and their workspace. They then integrate a Sphero Indi robot as the 'helper'?decorating it to match their creation?and design a hands-on challenge course using cardboard and props that simulates a real task (e.g., delivering books, watering plants, helping students cross a 'street'). Students use color tiles to code the Indi to complete the helper's job, iterating on both the physical course and their coding as they test and refine their designs. The process follows the Design Cycle: Imagine (choose a role and brainstorm), Plan (sketch and gather materials), Create (build and code), and Improve (test and tweak).

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Everyone has a role to play in the community' by investigating roles within their school, creating wonderings, interviewing staff, and sharing their learning through videos and presentations. Activities include guided walks, role-play, collaborative projects, and reflecting on how individuals contribute to the community.

ci: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.
lo: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

s.d: Students use Sky Map on iPads to explore the day and night sky, identifying the sun, moon, and stars as part of their inquiry into scientific observation. They track changes over time and connect their real-world sky observations to digital constellations, sparking curiosity about patterns and cycles in nature.

Empower students to document their Nature Detective Walk and Weather Watchers activities by capturing photos and short videos with the iPad Camera, then uploading them to Seesaw. Students can annotate their observations with voice recordings or drawings, making their scientific thinking visible and accessible to families.

After the Ice Melting Challenge or Rolling Ramp Investigation, students use ChatterPix Kids to animate photos of their experiment materials or 'scientist characters,' recording their own voices to explain predictions, results, and conclusions in a playful, memorable way.

Guide students to create a collaborative digital science journal in Book Creator, combining their drawings, photos, and voice recordings from each weekly experiment (nature walk, weather chart, ice melting, ramps, mystery materials). This living document showcases their journey through the scientific method.

Have students use ScratchJr to visually sequence the steps of a science experiment (e.g., the process of melting ice or rolling objects down ramps). They can animate simple characters to model 'predict, test, observe, record,' reinforcing the scientific process through coding and storytelling.

Students work in teams to design and build imaginative 'weather machines' that demonstrate a chosen weather phenomenon (rain, wind, or snow) using recycled materials, cardboard tubes, fabric, and craft supplies. For example, a rain machine might use a hand-cranked mechanism to drop 'raindrops' (beads or pom-poms), while a wind machine could use a fan made from paper and a small motor (with adult help). Once built, students use Bee-Bots to 'deliver' weather reports by programming them to travel between different weather stations on a large, collaboratively painted map. Students test, refine, and present how their machines mimic real weather, documenting their process with photos and simple voice recordings on iPads.

plannerText: Prep students explore the scientific method through hands-on experiences such as nature detective walks, weather observations, ice melting challenges, ramp investigations, and mystery material explorations. The unit emphasizes observing, recording, predicting, testing, and drawing conclusions, fostering inquiry and scientific thinking in authentic, age-appropriate contexts.

ci: Living things share our world and have an important role in nature
lo: The different living things around us; The roles living things play in nature; How humans and other living things share space
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Relationships impact our wellbeing.
lo: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Keeping ourselves healthy and safe

s.d: Students step into a Green Screen studio to create short videos where they act out different friendship scenarios that show how relationships impact wellbeing. Using simple props and backgrounds, they bring to life moments of sharing, helping, and caring, then watch their scenes transform with colourful, imaginative settings that highlight emotions and balance in friendships.

Students compose and record original soundtracks in GarageBand to represent different emotions and relationship scenarios. They experiment with tempo, instruments, and sound effects to express feelings like happiness, frustration, or calm, then share their audio creations as part of class discussions about wellbeing and friendship.

Collaboratively build a digital class book in Book Creator titled 'Our Friendship Stories.' Each student adds a page with a drawing or photo of themselves with a friend, accompanied by a short audio recording describing how their relationship makes them feel and why it is important for their wellbeing.

Use PicCollage on iPads for students to create vibrant visual collages showing 'What friendship looks like at school.' They can combine photos, stickers, and emojis to represent acts of kindness, teamwork, and support, then present their collages to the class as conversation starters.

Guide students to code simple animations in ScratchJr that show two characters navigating a playground scenario?such as sharing, resolving a conflict, or inviting someone to play?demonstrating how positive relationships impact wellbeing. Students can narrate their stories and share them with peers.

Students work in small groups to design and build a miniature playground using recycled materials, cardboard, popsticks, and clay, with each playground area representing a different feeling or positive social interaction (e.g., 'Kindness Slide', 'Sharing Sandbox', 'Helping Hand Swing'). Once constructed, students use Sphero Indi cars decorated as 'Friend Bots' to navigate the playground, coding simple colour paths that represent different friendship scenarios (e.g., Indi drives to the 'Kindness Slide' when students code a path for helping a friend). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by discussing playground experiences, ideate playground zones for positive relationships, prototype and build, test their Indi's journey, and reflect on how these spaces and actions foster wellbeing and friendships.

plannerText: The Prep 'Who We Are' unit explores how relationships impact wellbeing, focusing on developing friendships, understanding how those around us influence our behaviours, and recognising balance in our interactions. Key activities include read-alouds, oral discussions, drawing emotions, role-play, and creating an essential agreement to foster positive classroom relationships.

ci: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.
lo: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Different ways we express emotions

s.d: Students create digital 'emotion avatars' in Delightex, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate their avatars with their own voices expressing different feelings. This app smash allows students to visually design a character and then bring it to life, sharing how emotions can be expressed through both art and voice in a playful, creative way.

Students document and reflect on their own emotional expressions by uploading photos, drawings, or short videos showing different feelings. Teachers can prompt students to capture moments throughout the day when they feel happy, sad, excited, or calm, and add voice recordings to explain their emotions.

Students collaboratively build a digital class book titled 'Our Feelings', where each page features a student's drawing or photo of an emotion, paired with their recorded explanation or a short story about when they felt that way. This supports multimodal expression and builds empathy as students learn about each other's feelings.

Students use Puppet Pals to create short animated skits where characters experience and express different emotions in various scenarios (e.g., losing a toy, making a new friend). This helps students practice recognising and communicating emotions through storytelling and role-play.

Students create visual emotion collages using PicCollage, combining photos of their own facial expressions, emojis, and colourful backgrounds to represent a range of feelings. They can label each collage with the emotion and share with the class, sparking discussion about how we show what we feel.

Students work in teams to design and build expressive 'emotion robots' using Sphero Indi cars as the moving base. First, they brainstorm and sketch how to represent different feelings (happy, sad, excited, etc.) through facial features, colors, and accessories. Then, they use cardboard, recycled materials, felt, googly eyes, and craft supplies to physically build and decorate 3D robot 'faces' and bodies that attach securely to the Sphero Indi. Each team codes simple color paths for their robot to follow, choreographing a 'parade' that expresses a sequence of emotions (e.g., Indi moves along a blue path for 'sad', then a yellow path for 'happy'). The project culminates in a class robot parade, with students explaining their design choices and how their robots' movements and appearance express different emotions, connecting hands-on making, coding, and social-emotional learning.

plannerText: This unit explores how emotions can be expressed in different ways, with students engaging in activities such as reading picture books about feelings, creating artworks and masks to represent emotions, participating in role-play and music to communicate feelings, and reflecting on their own and others' emotional expressions through group discussions and visual projects.

ci: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.
lo: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and community needs

s.d: Students create short video skits using the green screen to showcase different community services in action. By placing themselves in digitally created settings?like a fire station or hospital?they act out scenarios that highlight how services help people, making their learning visible and engaging for peers.

Learners document a 'Day in the Life' of a chosen community helper by capturing photos or short videos using the iPad camera, then annotating with voice recordings and drawings in Seesaw. This creates a multimedia portfolio that showcases their understanding of the roles and importance of local services.

Students create a class digital book titled 'Our Community Heroes', where each page spotlights a different local service. They use Book Creator's drawing, audio, and photo features to visually represent how each service supports the community, making the learning accessible and engaging for Year 1.

Children take photos of community helper puppets or drawings, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short messages as if spoken by the helpers. This brings the roles of community workers to life and encourages empathy and communication skills.

Students go on a 'Community Services Scavenger Hunt' around the school or local area, taking photos with iPads of people and places that provide services. They then create vibrant collages in PicCollage, labeling each service and sharing their findings with the class.

Students work in teams to design and build a miniature 'community' using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, creating key locations like a fire station, hospital, and post office. Each team chooses a community helper (e.g., firefighter, paramedic, postal worker) and invents a rescue or service scenario (e.g., delivering medicine, putting out a small fire). Using Bee-Bots with custom-decorated shells matching their helper, students program routes to navigate their helpers through the physical town, overcoming crafted obstacles and delivering aid. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise with community needs, define a helper's mission, ideate and build the town, prototype and test Bee-Bot routes, and refine their designs for a final demonstration to the class.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 1 students explore how services are established to help meet the needs of people in the community. Through read-alouds, guest speakers, research, and presentations, students investigate local services, their roles, and how they support the community. Learning experiences include interviews, creative presentations, and writing appreciation letters to community helpers.

ci: Patterns help us to interpret the world around us.
lo: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to design and navigate pattern mazes that represent natural cycles, such as the water cycle or life cycles. By programming color-coded paths, they physically model and explain how patterns repeat in the world around us, reinforcing their understanding through hands-on exploration.

Students create talking pattern creatures by drawing or photographing patterns (e.g., butterfly wings, water cycle stages), then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with their own voices explaining the pattern or cycle. This brings abstract concepts to life in a playful, memorable way.

Facilitate a digital pattern journal in Seesaw where students document daily or weekly observations of patterns and cycles (e.g., weather, classroom routines, plant growth), using photos, voice recordings, and drawings to reflect on how these patterns help us interpret the world.

Guide students to collaboratively build a digital class book in Book Creator, with each child contributing a page about a different pattern or cycle (e.g., life cycle of a butterfly, days of the week, repeating shapes in art). Pages can include photos, drawings, and audio explanations, making learning multimodal and accessible.

Students design simple animations in ScratchJr to model and extend repeating patterns or cycles (e.g., day-night, seasons, animal life cycles). They can program characters to move, change, or transform in a sequence, visually demonstrating their understanding of how patterns repeat and change.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, 3D diorama representing the four seasons or a life cycle (e.g., butterfly, frog) using cardboard, clay, fabric scraps, and recycled materials. They incorporate simple mechanisms (e.g., rotating discs, sliders, pop-up elements) to show changes over time. Using Bee-Bots, students code simple paths that travel through their diorama, triggering physical events (like a flower blooming or a sun rising) at each station. Teams use iPads to record short videos explaining the patterns and cycles demonstrated in their interactive model, blending creative building, basic robotics, and digital storytelling.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring patterns and cycles in the natural world and daily life, including life cycles, seasons, and the water cycle. Students engage in hands-on investigations, digital research, and creative activities to observe, document, and interpret patterns, building critical thinking and research skills while making connections across science, art, and literacy.

ci: Plants are a life sustaining resource.
lo: Products derived from plants; Plants all around us; The interconnectedness of plants

s.d: Learners program Sphero Indi cars to travel through a garden-themed obstacle course, representing the journey of a plant from seed to flower. As they navigate each stage, students explain plant needs and life cycle changes, blending coding with science and storytelling.

Students photograph different plants and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the plants, giving them a 'voice' to explain their role in sustaining life. This empowers students to creatively demonstrate their understanding of plant functions and importance in an engaging, age-appropriate way.

Facilitate a collaborative digital 'Plant Life Cycle Storybook' using Book Creator. Each student contributes a page with drawings, photos, and voice narration about a stage in a plant's life cycle, resulting in a multimedia class book that documents their inquiry journey.

Guide students to collect leaves, petals, or seeds, photograph them with the iPad, and create digital plant collages in PicCollage. They then upload their creations to Seesaw, adding voice reflections about how each part helps plants sustain life, fostering both creativity and scientific thinking.

Students design simple animations showing the journey of a seed growing into a plant, including the resources it needs (sun, water, soil). This visual coding activity helps solidify understanding of plant life cycles and the concept of plants as life-sustaining resources.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, 3D garden model using recycled cardboard, fabric scraps, bottle caps, and clay to represent different stages of plant growth (seed, sprout, mature plant, flower, fruit). They embed simple circuits (using battery packs, LED lights, and copper tape) to illuminate parts of the garden that represent key life stages, such as a glowing seed or a blooming flower. Using iPads, students record short audio explanations for each stage and attach them to their models with QR codes (generated with a QR code app). When scanned, these codes play the students' own voices explaining what happens at each stage. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate plant needs, brainstorm and sketch designs, build prototypes, test the circuits and QR codes, and share their interactive garden with peers and families.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring how plants are a life-sustaining resource. Through hands-on investigations, digital research, and creative projects, students observe plant life cycles, document changes, and communicate their understanding using visual and tactile digital tools. The learning is grounded in real-world observations and student agency, with strong links to science, literacy, and transdisciplinary inquiry.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Homes reflect our culture and environment.
lo: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainable living spaces

s.d: Students use the green screen to create video tours of homes from different cultures and environments. By placing themselves in front of digital backdrops, they narrate the unique features and stories of each home, making global connections come alive for the class.

Invite students to draw or photograph different types of homes (e.g., yurts, igloos, apartments) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the homes, giving them a 'voice' to share facts about their culture, environment, and sustainability features in a fun, accessible way.

Guide students to collaboratively build a digital class book titled 'Homes Around the World', where each child creates a page about a unique home type, adding photos, drawings, audio reflections, and simple labels, supporting both literacy and cultural understanding.

Facilitate a 'Home Design Reflection Journal' on Seesaw, where students document their learning journey by uploading photos of their mini home builds, voice-recording their design choices, and responding to prompts about how their home reflects their culture or environment.

Have students create vibrant visual collages comparing two types of homes (e.g., a houseboat and a cave home) using PicCollage, incorporating images, stickers, and simple text to highlight similarities and differences in structure, materials, and cultural significance.

Students work in teams to research and select a type of home from a different culture or environment (e.g., igloo, stilt house, mud hut, yurt). Using recycled materials, cardboard, fabric, and clay, they build detailed mini models of these homes, ensuring features reflect their chosen culture and climate. Next, students program Sphero Indi cars with colour tiles or Bee-Bots with directional cards to 'visit' each home, creating a physical map with paths connecting the models. As the robots travel, students narrate or record short facts about each home using iPads, which classmates can listen to at each stop. This hands-on project integrates design, building, basic robotics, and cultural storytelling, culminating in an interactive showcase where peers and families can explore the village and learn about global diversity.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 1 students explore how homes reflect culture and environment by investigating a variety of global home types, building mini models, engaging in comparative discussions, and showcasing their learning through creative projects and a learning showcase. The unit emphasizes hands-on inquiry, cultural appreciation, and student agency through open-ended building and reflection tasks.

ci: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing.
lo: Safe environments; How our actions affect ourselves and others; Keeping our body and mind healthy

s.d: Students create a 'Healthy Habits Hero' by drawing themselves or a character making a safe or healthy choice (e.g., washing hands, wearing a helmet). Using ChatterPix Kids, they animate the character to give a spoken safety or wellbeing tip, then upload their video to Seesaw for a class 'Wellbeing Gallery'. This app-smash empowers student voice and makes health messages memorable and fun.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'My Healthy Body Book', adding photos, drawings, and voice recordings about ways to keep their bodies and minds safe and healthy. Each page can focus on a different aspect: nutrition, exercise, sleep, or emotional wellbeing, allowing for highly visual, multimodal expression.

Students design obstacle courses for Sphero Indi cars that represent healthy and unhealthy choices in daily routines. As they navigate the course, they explain how different actions?like eating well or exercising?help keep our bodies safe and well, making abstract concepts tangible and fun.

Learners go on a 'Safety Scavenger Hunt' around the school, using the iPad camera to capture examples of safe and unsafe practices (e.g., tidy vs. messy walkways, correct vs. incorrect handwashing). They assemble their photos into a PicCollage poster, labeling each with emojis or simple icons to reinforce visual learning.

Students design simple animations in ScratchJr where characters make choices that affect their wellbeing (e.g., choosing to eat fruit, wear sunscreen, or take a break when tired). They can program cause-and-effect sequences, deepening their understanding of how actions influence outcomes.

Students work in teams to design and build a life-sized human body outline using cardboard, fabric scraps, recycled containers, and craft materials. They create removable, tactile 'organ' pieces (e.g., heart, lungs, stomach) using clay, felt, and other textured supplies. Each organ is fitted with a simple conductive material (aluminum foil, copper tape) and connected to a Makey Makey kit and iPad running a pre-made interactive soundboard (e.g., GarageBand or a recorded Keynote). When an organ is placed correctly, it completes a circuit and triggers a sound or student-recorded message about how that body part contributes to safety and wellbeing (e.g., 'The heart keeps your blood moving!'). Students follow the Design Cycle: empathize (explore their own bodies and healthy choices), define (choose key organs and messages), ideate (sketch and plan), prototype (build and wire organs), test (try with classmates), and share (present their interactive body board at the Healthy Bodies Museum).

plannerText: This unit explores how the actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing, with students engaging in activities such as daily read-alouds about the human body, building life-size body models, participating in healthy habits inquiry stations, and creating informative books about body parts and healthy choices. The learning is highly hands-on and visual, connecting to real-life experiences like the swimming program and culminating in student-led presentations and a Healthy Bodies Museum.

ci: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity.
lo: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating art

s.d: Students become artists and performers by recording themselves presenting their creative works using the green screen. They choose digital backgrounds that match the mood or theme of their art, poetry, or music, and share their imaginative expressions in a class 'virtual gallery' event.

Students photograph their own artworks or creative projects, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate their creations and give them a 'voice' to explain their inspiration and artistic choices. These talking artworks are then compiled into a digital class gallery using Book Creator, celebrating diverse forms of expression and imagination.

Learners document their creative process by capturing photos and videos of their artwork, poetry, and music explorations in Seesaw. They use drawing and voice annotation tools to reflect on how their imagination and appreciation of others' work has grown, building a rich, multimedia portfolio of their artistic journey.

Students use Canva's highly visual templates to design vibrant posters that showcase different forms of artistic expression (art, poetry, music, dance). They combine their own photos, drawings, and Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to highlight how creativity and imagination can be communicated in unique ways.

Using Puppet Pals on iPads, students create and voice original short plays or fairy tales, experimenting with character, emotion, and perspective. This hands-on digital puppetry encourages risk-taking and empathy as students bring their imaginative stories to life and share them with the class.

s.t: Emotion Machines: Build a Moving Sculpture
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a kinetic 'Emotion Machine'?a moving sculpture that visually represents a chosen feeling (e.g., joy, surprise, calm) using cardboard, recycled materials, pipe cleaners, fabric, and craft supplies. They use simple robotics kits (e.g., Sphero Indi or basic motors with battery packs) to add motion or light to their sculpture, such as spinning parts, waving arms, or glowing sections. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by discussing how different emotions look and feel, ideate creative ways to express these through movement and materials, prototype their machines, test and refine the movement, and finally present their sculptures, explaining how the physical and digital elements together express their chosen emotion.

plannerText: This unit explores how artistic appreciation and expressions inspire imagination and creativity in Year 2 students. Learners engage with diverse forms of expression?including art, poetry, and music?through hands-on activities, collaborative projects, and reflection, building empathy, risk-taking, and appreciation for their own and others' creativity.

ci: Marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities.
lo: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to simulate a marketplace, programming their cars to travel between 'shops' and 'customers' on a classroom map. As they navigate, they discuss needs and wants, and how products move through a community, making economic concepts interactive and memorable.

Students photograph objects from home or the classroom that represent a 'need' or 'want', then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the objects and record short persuasive pitches explaining why their item is essential or desirable in a marketplace. These can be shared in a class 'Marketplace Gallery' to spark discussion about value and perspective.

Create a collaborative digital 'Needs & Wants Marketplace Book' where each student authors a page featuring a product they designed to meet a specific community need. Pages include photos, drawings, and voice recordings, making the book highly visual and accessible for Year 2 learners.

Facilitate a 'Market Research Collage' activity: students visit a local market (virtually or in person), take photos using iPad cameras, and use PicCollage to sort and label items as 'needs' or 'wants'. They can add stickers and voice notes to explain their reasoning, then present their findings to the class.

Guide students to create simple animated stories in ScratchJr where characters must make choices between needs and wants in a marketplace scenario. Students program their characters to interact, negotiate, and reflect on the consequences of their decisions, reinforcing the central idea through creative coding.

s.t: Marketplace Pop-Up Stalls with Interactive Robots
s.d: Students work in teams to identify a real need or want within their school community. Each team designs and builds a physical 'pop-up stall' using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies to represent a marketplace booth offering their product or service. To add an interactive tech element, students program a Bee-Bot or Blue-Bot to act as a 'customer' or 'delivery robot' that visits their stall. Teams must design the booth with ramps, gates, or simple mechanisms (e.g., levers, flaps) that the robot can trigger to 'receive' a product or reveal information. The project integrates the design cycle: empathizing with community needs, ideating solutions, prototyping both the stall and interactive robot pathway, and testing with peers. This hands-on project deepens understanding of needs vs. wants and how marketplaces function, while blending digital programming with physical engineering and creativity.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 2 students explore how marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities, with a strong focus on distinguishing between needs and wants, understanding changing community needs, and using the design process to create solutions. Activities include role-play, sorting games, design thinking, and a culminating project where students design products to meet real community needs.

ci: Forces and motion are all around us.
lo: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

s.d: Learners design and test Sphero Indi obstacle courses that demonstrate different forces?like push, pull, and friction?at work. As they adjust the course and observe the car?s movement, students explain how each force affects motion, turning abstract science into hands-on discovery.

s.t: App Smashing: iPad Camera + ChatterPix Kids + Seesaw
s.d: Students take photos around the school playground of real-life examples of forces (e.g., swings, slides, ramps) using the iPad Camera. They then use ChatterPix Kids to animate these objects, having them 'talk' about the forces acting on them, before uploading their creations to Seesaw for class sharing and reflection.

Students design and code simple animations or games that demonstrate push, pull, gravity, and friction. For example, they can create a scene where a character moves differently on ice versus grass, or where a ball is pushed up and down a ramp, visually exploring the effects of different forces.

Learners create interactive digital books documenting their experiments with forces and motion. Each page can feature photos, voice recordings, and drawings showing their predictions, observations, and conclusions from hands-on activities like car ramps, magnet mazes, and friction tests.

Students create vibrant, visual collages comparing different types of forces and simple machines. They can use photos from their playground walk, stickers, and labels to sort and classify examples of push, pull, friction, and gravity, then present their collages to the class.

Students work in teams to design and build intricate vertical marble mazes using cardboard, recycled tubes, popsticks, and connectors, incorporating ramps, levers, and pulleys to explore forces, motion, and simple machines. Using iPads with the Stop Motion Studio app, students record marbles traveling through their mazes, annotate key force interactions (push, pull, gravity, friction), and reflect on their design process. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate how marbles move, plan their maze layout, build and test prototypes, improve their designs based on marble performance, and present their final creations as interactive displays, blending hands-on engineering with digital documentation and analysis.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in hands-on investigations of forces and motion, including push, pull, gravity, friction, and simple machines. Students explore these concepts through playground observations, experiments with ramps and magnets, creative projects like Rube Goldberg machines, and reflective activities that connect scientific thinking to real-world play and innovation.

ci: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make.
lo: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our planet

s.d: Transform your 'Sharing the Planet' unit by having Year 2 students create interactive digital posters in Canva! After investigating Earth's resources and conducting waste audits, students can design vibrant, persuasive posters that showcase practical ways to care for the planet. Using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature, they can add photos of their own eco-friendly actions (taken with the iPad Camera app), then layer in fun graphics, facts, and their own voice recordings. This hands-on project empowers students to become environmental advocates, sharing their learning with the school community in a visually engaging, creative way.

App smash ChatterPix Kids and PicCollage: Students photograph items from their lunchboxes or classroom, use ChatterPix to make the items 'speak' about their environmental impact, and then create a visual collage advocating for better choices. This empowers students to become persuasive advocates for Earth-friendly habits in a playful, creative way.

Students create interactive digital books documenting their week-long 'Waste Detective' investigation, including photos of lunchbox waste, graphs of recyclable vs. landfill items, and voice recordings reflecting on how their choices affect the environment. These books can be shared with families to inspire action beyond the classroom.

Facilitate a 'Global Earth Care Challenge' where students document daily actions that help the planet (e.g., turning off lights, composting, picking up litter) with photos and short audio reflections in Seesaw. Connect with a partner class at another Wesley campus to compare and celebrate sustainable choices.

Students embark on the 'Ocean Heroes' Minecraft Education adventure (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/ocean-heroes), rescuing coral reefs and learning real-world conservation skills. This hands-on digital journey directly links to the central idea by showing how individual and collective choices protect Earth's resources.

Students work in teams to design and build model animal shelters using recycled materials (cardboard, fabric scraps, plastic containers) that address a specific environmental threat (e.g., heat, rain, pollution). Using Sphero Indi or Bee-Bots with simple coding, students integrate a 'smart' feature: for example, programming the robot to simulate an animal seeking shelter or triggering a sensor (like a paper flap that opens when the robot arrives). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize by researching local animals and their needs, ideate and sketch solutions, prototype with tangible materials, test with the robot, and reflect on how their design helps both animals and the planet. This hands-on, tech-integrated build encourages creative problem-solving and environmental stewardship.

plannerText: The Year 2 'Sharing the Planet' unit centers on the idea that caring for Earth begins with our choices. Students investigate Earth's resources, analyze how their actions impact the environment, and explore practical ways to care for the planet through hands-on inquiry, waste audits, persuasive projects, and community action.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.
lo: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between landmarks and identity; How landmarks and symbols represent our history

s.d: Transport your Year 2 students on a virtual journey through Australia's most significant landmarks and places of cultural importance using Delightex's immersive 3D scenes. Students can collaboratively build and explore digital dioramas of local and national sites, layering in Indigenous perspectives, historical facts, and their own creative storytelling. This hands-on, inquiry-driven experience empowers learners to 'walk through' the stories behind monuments and symbols, deepening their understanding of place, culture, and memory. For their final presentation, students can capture their digital dioramas and create a vibrant, multimodal showcase using Canva, sharing their discoveries and reflections with the class.

Students embark on a virtual exploration using Google Maps to investigate local and global landmarks, tracing the stories and histories behind each site. They drop pins on significant places, annotate with facts and images, and create a digital 'landmark tour' that connects their personal experiences to the broader narrative of place and time. This interactive mapping brings the central idea?how landmarks tell stories?vividly to life.

Students create a collaborative digital class book in Book Creator, with each child designing a page about a landmark or place of significance (local or global). Pages can include photos, drawings, voice recordings, and simple maps, allowing for multimodal expression and easy sharing with families.

Using Bee-Bots and custom-made floor maps, students program their robots to 'visit' different landmarks placed on a classroom map of Australia or the world. Each stop includes a quick fact or story, reinforcing mapping skills, sequencing, and the connection between place and story.

Students design vibrant digital posters in Canva, showcasing a chosen landmark's story, cultural significance, and a key symbol. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add themselves into the scene, making the learning personal and visually engaging.

Students work in teams to research and select local or national landmarks, including those significant to Indigenous communities. Using air-dry clay, recycled materials, and paints, they physically build miniature 3D models of these landmarks. For each landmark, students also craft a 'story stone'?a painted stone embedded with a simple NFC tag or QR code sticker. They record their own short audio stories or digital slideshows about the landmark's history, meaning, or a retelling of a related Indigenous story using an iPad app. The digital content is linked to the NFC/QR code. The class arranges the models on a large collaborative map of Australia made from cardboard and fabric, creating an 'Interactive Landmarks Trail.' Peers and visitors can physically walk the trail, scan each story stone with an iPad, and experience the student-created digital stories alongside the tactile models, blending tech and hands-on making to explore place, culture, and memory.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in exploring how landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history. Through mapping, storytelling, Indigenous perspectives, and hands-on inquiry, learners connect with local and national sites, investigate the meaning behind symbols and monuments, and create multimodal representations to deepen their understanding of place, culture, and memory.

ci: Our relationships with others impact our lives.
lo: Importance of relationships; Developing and maintaining healthy relationships; The impact of relationships on wellbeing

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to model positive relationship-building scenarios, programming the car to navigate a path representing steps in friendship or conflict resolution. As they guide Indi through each stage, learners discuss how their choices and actions impact relationships, making social-emotional learning concrete and playful.

Students take photos of themselves or classroom objects and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, giving voice to different perspectives in a friendship scenario. For example, they can make a lunchbox 'talk' about how it feels when shared or not shared, directly linking to the central idea of relationships impacting lives.

Create a collaborative digital class book in Book Creator where each student adds a page about a time when a relationship (friend, family, teacher, pet) made a difference in their life. Students can use photos, drawings, and voice recordings to make it highly visual and personal.

Students design and act out short digital puppet shows in Puppet Pals, dramatizing real-life playground or classroom scenarios that require empathy, problem-solving, or conflict resolution. This helps them practice and reflect on healthy relationship skills in a creative, low-pressure way.

Have students create a visual 'Friendship Recipe' in PicCollage, combining photos, stickers, and drawings to represent the ingredients of a healthy relationship (e.g., kindness, listening, sharing). They can share their recipes with the class and discuss how each 'ingredient' impacts their daily interactions.

Students work in small groups to design and build miniature 'Friendship Forts' using cardboard, fabric scraps, recycled boxes, and craft materials. Each fort represents a unique community space that encourages positive relationships. Students embed simple interactive tech?such as Makey Makey kits or micro:bit touch sensors?so that touching or connecting certain parts of their fort triggers digital responses (e.g., playing a recorded message about kindness, lighting up an LED 'welcome' sign, or playing a friendship song). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by discussing what makes people feel welcome and included, define features their fort should have, ideate and sketch, physically prototype, test with classmates, and iterate based on feedback. The final showcase is a 'Friendship Village', where students connect their forts with bridges or tunnels, symbolising healthy connections between people and groups.

plannerText: This year-long Year 2 unit explores how our relationships with others impact our lives, focusing on the importance of relationships, developing and maintaining healthy connections, and understanding the changeable nature of relationships. Students engage in activities that build social-emotional intelligence, empathy, and cooperative skills, supported by explicit teaching of self-regulation, friendship strategies, and reflective practices.

ci: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.
lo: The ways personal and cultural events are celebrated; Our appreciation of the diversity of cultures; How celebrations shape community identity

s.t: Minecraft: Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage
s.d: Immerse your Year 3 students in a vibrant exploration of global celebrations and traditions by using Minecraft: Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage. In this lesson, students will virtually visit and help protect famous cultural heritage sites from around the world, discovering how communities express their identities through celebrations, architecture, and shared stories. They'll collaborate to build their own representations of Australian multicultural events, reflecting on what it means to be a global citizen. This hands-on, creative experience will deepen their appreciation for diversity and the importance of preserving cultural traditions.

Students create interactive digital infographics or posters in Canva that showcase the unique traditions, foods, music, and symbols of their own or a peer's cultural background, integrating images, voice recordings, and embedded videos to bring their research to life.

Facilitate a collaborative digital book in Book Creator where each student authors a page about a celebration or tradition from their family or heritage, including photos, drawings, and audio explanations. The final book can be shared with the school community or families.

Guide students to document their family traditions by interviewing family members using the iPad Camera app, then upload and annotate their videos, photos, and voice notes in Seesaw. Encourage students to comment on each other's posts to foster intercultural understanding.

Challenge students to design a short, animated video or digital story in Adobe Express that explains the significance of a celebration or tradition, using music, narration, and visual effects to engage their audience.

Students work in teams to select a cultural celebration from around the world. Using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, they design and build a physical parade float that represents key symbols and traditions of their chosen celebration. Then, they integrate Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot robots by designing a coded path for the robot to 'drive' the float through a custom parade route. Students decorate the route with handmade props and signs reflecting multicultural Australia. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize by researching celebrations, define their float's purpose, ideate creative features, prototype with craft materials, program the robot, test the parade, and reflect on how their float expresses cultural identity and diversity.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore how celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community by investigating personal and global cultural events, appreciating diversity, and reflecting on what it means to be a global citizen. Activities include mind mapping traditions, researching and presenting cultural identities, creating digital presentations, and collaborating on creative projects that highlight the richness of multicultural Australia.

ci: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community.
lo: The differences between rules and laws; People responsible for enforcing rules and laws; Rights and responsibilities of citizens; The consequences when rules, laws and rights are not met

s.d: Transform your 'How We Organise Ourselves' unit by having students collaboratively design immersive 3D scenes that explore the differences between rules and laws, the people who enforce them, and the rights and responsibilities of citizens. Students can create virtual town squares, courtrooms, or global communities, populating them with avatars and interactive elements that represent real-world scenarios. This hands-on, creative approach deepens understanding of citizenship by allowing students to 'step into' the roles of lawmakers, citizens, and enforcers, and reflect on the consequences when rules or rights are not upheld. For their final presentation, students can use Canva to showcase screenshots and reflections from their Delightex worlds, making their learning visible and engaging for the whole class.

Students design persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for a new school rule or community law, using Canva's templates and visual tools to communicate the importance of rights and responsibilities. These can be shared in a virtual gallery for peer voting and feedback.

Create interactive digital storybooks in Book Creator where students write and illustrate stories about characters navigating rules, laws, and consequences in different communities. Incorporate voice recordings and clickable elements for an engaging, multimodal experience.

Students script and record a podcast series debating the differences between rules and laws, and the responsibilities of citizens in various global contexts. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they interview classmates, role-play as lawmakers, and reflect on how rules shape communities, bringing real-world issues to life through engaging audio storytelling.

Students explore the 'Active Citizen' Minecraft Education world to learn about Nobel Peace Laureates and how laws and rights shape communities. They can build their own community spaces and propose laws, reflecting on the impact of their decisions. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/active-citizen

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, interactive board game that teaches the difference between rules and laws, the roles of enforcers, and the rights and responsibilities of citizens. Using recycled cardboard, craft supplies, and markers, students build a 3D game board with physical spaces, pop-up features, and hand-crafted player pieces. Integrated with tech, students program Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot robots as 'game movers', coding them to follow player turns and respond to special cards or tiles (e.g., 'Break a rule?go back 2 spaces', 'Follow a law?move forward'). Teams ideate, prototype, test, and refine their games, ensuring the mechanics reinforce citizenship concepts. The project culminates in a showcase where students teach others to play, reflecting on how their design choices represent real-world systems of rules, laws, and community responsibility.

plannerText: This unit engages students in exploring the differences between rules and laws, the people responsible for enforcing them, and the rights and responsibilities of citizens. Activities include creating persuasive texts, researching global laws, and reflecting on the consequences when rules and rights are not met. Students connect learning to local and global contexts, culminating in collaborative and creative projects that deepen their understanding of citizenship.

ci: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.
lo: The work of scientists; The process of learning; How science brings about new understandings and changes

s.d: Invite Year 3 students to step into a virtual museum of human ingenuity! In this hands-on, creative experience, students will explore key scientific inventions throughout history, then use their scientific understanding to design and build their own invention in Minecraft. They'll research a scientist or invention connected to the UN Sustainable Development Goals, reflect on how scientific principles drive innovation, and present their creation using Canva or Adobe Express. This immersive activity empowers students to see themselves as inventors, connecting classroom science to real-world action and global challenges.

Students create dynamic infographics or animated posters in Canva to showcase the biography and scientific achievements of a chosen scientist, highlighting how their understanding of scientific principles led to real-world action. These can be displayed digitally or printed for a science fair, directly connecting to the unit's focus on scientists' impact.

Have students document their own science experiments and investigations by creating interactive digital science journals in Book Creator. They can include photos, diagrams, voice recordings, and reflections on how they used scientific principles to solve problems or take action, mirroring the scientific method explored in the unit.

Students use Micro:bit to conduct hands-on experiments with sensors and data collection, deepening their understanding of how scientific principles are applied to real-world problems. By engaging with the 'Computing Fundamentals' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/computing-fundamentals/), learners design simple investigations, collect data, and reflect on how technology empowers scientists to take action for the UN Sustainable Development Goals.

Students apply their understanding of scientific measurement by designing and solving real-world area and volume challenges in Minecraft Education. This lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/area-and-volume-2) lets students experiment with scale and space, linking directly to the unit's focus on scientific principles and their applications.

Students will work in teams to identify a real-world environmental problem (e.g., clean water, energy, waste) linked to the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Using the Design Cycle, each team will brainstorm, sketch, and physically prototype an innovative solution using recycled materials, cardboard, popsticks, and craft supplies. To integrate technology, students will embed simple circuits (using LED bulbs, coin batteries, and conductive tape) or programmable elements (like Sphero Indi or Bee-Bots) into their models to demonstrate how their invention works. For example, a team designing a water filter might build a physical model and use LED circuits to show water flow, or a team addressing recycling could create an interactive sorting machine powered by a Sphero Indi navigating through their crafted structure. Projects will be presented in a 'Sustainable Solutions Showcase' where students explain their scientific thinking, the design process, and how their invention could make a difference in the real world.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 3 students in exploring how humans use scientific principles to take action, focusing on the work of scientists, the process of scientific learning, and how science brings about new understandings. Students conduct hands-on experiments, research scientists, create science fair projects, and connect their learning to real-world issues and the UN Sustainable Development Goals.

ci: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments.
lo: Adaptations and structural features of living things; Roles of living things in an ecosystem; Actions that benefit or harm ecosystems

s.t: Minecraft: Planet Earth Iii Lesson Collection2
s.d: Immerse Year 3 students in real-world animal adaptation and sustainability by exploring diverse biomes and playing as different animals in Minecraft. Students will investigate how creatures like the Cape fur seal, great white shark, musk ox, and Arctic wolf adapt to their environments and respond to human impacts. This interactive experience allows students to witness adaptation in action, understand ecosystem roles, and reflect on how sustainability ensures survival in changing environments. Students can document their discoveries and present their team-based findings using Canva or Adobe Express, directly linking to the UN sustainability goals.

Students collaboratively design digital infographics or animated posters about animal adaptations and ecosystem threats, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create visually striking, information-rich presentations for their documentaries and assembly presentations.

Learners produce interactive digital books documenting their research on animal adaptations, food webs, and human impacts on ecosystems. These books can include embedded videos, audio reflections, and student-drawn diagrams, supporting multimodal learning and sharing with the school community.

Teams create a wildlife adaptation podcast, narrating stories of animal survival and human impact on ecosystems. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), students blend research, interviews, and sound effects to bring their documentaries to life, culminating in an audio showcase that highlights sustainability and adaptation.

Learners employ Micro:bit to investigate animal adaptations and sustainability by creating digital models that simulate environmental changes and their effects on living things. Using the 'Helping Plants Grow' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/helping-plants-grow/), students design experiments to monitor plant growth and environmental conditions, connecting digital data to real-world sustainability challenges.

Students work in teams to design and build interactive dioramas that showcase an animal's unique adaptations for survival in a specific ecosystem. Using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and craft supplies, groups construct detailed habitats and create movable animal models. Each diorama features at least one simple mechanical element (e.g., levers, pulleys) to demonstrate how the adaptation functions (like a chameleon's tongue or a bird's beak). Students integrate tech by embedding a simple circuit (using Makey Makey or conductive tape and micro-switches) that triggers a recorded animal fact or adaptation explanation via an iPad or laptop when a habitat feature is touched. The project follows the Design Cycle: students research, plan, prototype, test, and refine their automata, then present their interactive creations to peers, explaining the adaptation's role in sustainability and survival.

plannerText: In this unit, students investigate how adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments. They explore animal adaptations, roles in ecosystems, and human impacts, culminating in team-based documentary projects, digital presentations, and action-oriented reflections aligned with UN sustainability goals.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society.
lo: The factors that motivate exploration (Causation); How people reflect on discoveries from the past to inform change (Change); How exploration brings about change and new understandings for people, places and society (Change)

s.d: Students will become 'Exploration News Reporters' and create dynamic multimedia presentations in Adobe Express, retelling the story of the First Fleet from multiple perspectives?including those of Indigenous Australians, convicts, and explorers. They will combine historical images, voice recordings, and text to highlight motivations for exploration, the impact on society, and connections to modern discoveries. This approach encourages empathy, critical thinking, and creativity, allowing students to make powerful connections between past and present explorations.

Students research a famous explorer or First Fleet figure, then create a dynamic infographic in Canva highlighting their motivations, discoveries, and impact. They upload their infographic to Seesaw and record an audio reflection in character, explaining how exploration shaped society from their perspective.

Students explore and build historically accurate homes from different eras and cultures, including those of First Nations Australians and early settlers. They reflect on how exploration and migration influenced architecture and daily life, then present their builds to the class.

Students create interactive digital journals from the perspective of a First Fleet convict, Indigenous Australian, or explorer. They include diary entries, maps, drawings, and voice recordings to document the journey, discoveries, and societal changes brought by exploration.

Using Google Maps, students plot the routes of famous explorers and the First Fleet, pinning key events and discoveries along the way. They then collaborate on a Padlet timeline, adding images, facts, and reflections about how each journey changed society.

Students work in teams to research a historical exploration (e.g., the First Fleet or another significant journey) and then design and build a large, physical 'time capsule maze' using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. The maze represents the journey of explorers, with obstacles and decision points inspired by real historical challenges and Indigenous perspectives. Students program a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot to navigate through their maze, symbolizing the explorer's path and choices. Along the route, students create interactive, lift-the-flap facts, miniature artifacts, and Indigenous viewpoints using clay or craft materials. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize with explorers and Indigenous Australians, define challenges, ideate maze layouts, prototype with materials, test and refine both their maze and robot navigation, and finally share their creations in a gallery walk, explaining the historical and societal impacts embedded in each maze element.

plannerText: This unit engages students in exploring how exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society, with a focus on the First Fleet, motivations for exploration, and the impact on Indigenous Australians and broader society. Students investigate historical and contemporary explorations, reflect on multiple perspectives, and create projects that connect past discoveries to future possibilities.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Transform your 'Who We Are' unit by having students design interactive 3D scenarios in Delightex that explore real-life challenges and setbacks. Students can collaboratively build virtual scenes where characters face dilemmas about online safety, resilience, and respectful friendships. They can then role-play solutions, reflect on growth mindset strategies, and demonstrate how to take responsibility for their own wellbeing. This immersive approach empowers student agency, deepens social-emotional learning, and makes abstract concepts tangible and memorable.

Students collaboratively design digital 'Growth Mindset Posters' in Canva, featuring their own strategies for overcoming setbacks, positive self-talk, and resilience tips. These can be displayed around the school and shared with families to reinforce the central idea of managing change and building perseverance.

Create interactive digital journals in Book Creator where students document real-life challenges, their emotional responses, and the strategies they used to manage change or resolve friendship fires. Include audio reflections and photos to make the journals personal and engaging.

Students design and host a Kahoot quiz game based on resilience strategies, growth vs. fixed mindset scenarios, and online/offline safety. This encourages peer teaching and reinforces key concepts from the unit in a fun, competitive format.

App smash Seesaw with iPad camera and ChatterPix Kids: Students record short video reflections or role-play scenarios where they demonstrate respectful friendship strategies or personal safety choices, then upload to Seesaw for peer feedback and self-assessment.

Students work in teams to design and build a physical obstacle course using recycled materials, cardboard, string, and simple craft supplies. Each obstacle represents a real-life challenge (e.g., friendship conflict, online safety, learning a new skill). Teams embed Sphero Indi cars or Bee-Bots as 'resilience robots' that must navigate the course, with students programming the robots to model positive strategies (e.g., pausing to reflect, asking for help). Along the way, students craft interactive checkpoints?such as spinning wheels with growth mindset affirmations, or bridges that open when a team shares a respectful friendship tip. The project follows the Design Cycle: empathize (identify common challenges), define (choose obstacles), ideate (brainstorm course and checkpoints), prototype (build and test obstacles), and reflect (journal and peer feedback on strategies for overcoming setbacks).

plannerText: This unit explores how individuals respond to challenges and setbacks, develop personal strategies to manage change, and take responsibility for their own safety both online and face-to-face. Students investigate growth mindset, resilience, and respectful friendships through reflective journals, scenario-based activities, and collaborative projects, with strong links to social-emotional learning and student agency.

ci: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.
lo: How technology expands creative possibilities; The evolution of communication tools; The impact of technology on expression

s.d: Ignite student creativity by having them explore the evolution of communication and creative expression in 'The Hall of Inventions'. Students will journey through a virtual museum of history's greatest innovations, then design and build their own communication tool or creative technology in Minecraft. This hands-on challenge empowers learners to reflect on how technology transforms the way we express ourselves, and to showcase their own ideas for the future. For their final presentation, students will document their invention and its impact using Canva or Adobe Express, connecting their digital creations to real-world communication and ethical practices.

Students design digital posters or infographics that compare the evolution of communication tools, highlighting how technology has expanded creative possibilities and changed the way we share ideas. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create dynamic, layered visuals without the need for green screen kits.

Learners author interactive digital books that showcase their own imaginative stories, poems, or informational texts, integrating audio, video, and images to demonstrate how technology enables new forms of creative expression and multimodal communication.

Students document a 'Day in the Life' digital storytelling project by capturing photos and short videos with the iPad Camera app, then curate and narrate their story in Seesaw. This workflow highlights how technology personalizes and amplifies student voice for authentic sharing with peers and families.

Create short, animated explainer videos on cybersafety or ethical digital citizenship, using Adobe Express to combine images, icons, and voiceover. Students reflect on how technology can be used responsibly to educate and inspire others.

Students work in teams to design and build their own interactive storytelling machines that blend technology and physical creativity. Using Sphero Indi robots, they craft a physical story map from recycled cardboard, clay, and craft materials, creating story 'scenes' and obstacles. Each group codes their Indi to travel through the map, triggering different story events or sound effects with coloured tiles and simple circuits (e.g., Makey Makey or battery-powered buzzers). Students prototype, test, and refine their machines using the Design Cycle, ensuring their story is engaging, interactive, and communicates a meaningful message about creative expression. The final machines are showcased to younger students, who can 'drive' the story themselves, connecting digital and physical forms of communication.

plannerText: This unit engages students in exploring how technology transforms creative expression and communication. Learners investigate the evolution of communication tools, ethical and cybersafe digital practices, and design and deliver technology workshops for younger students, culminating in authentic action that connects their learning to the wider school community.

ci: Systems connect us.
lo: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on communities

s.d: Dive into the fascinating world of human-made systems by exploring how wastewater travels from homes to treatment plants in 'Outflow Order.' Students will investigate each part of the water treatment system, map the connections between components, and analyse how changes or disruptions impact the entire process. This immersive, hands-on Minecraft experience perfectly complements your water cycle experiments and systems mapping, allowing students to visualise, build, and test real-world systems in a collaborative digital environment. For their final presentation, students will use Canva to create a dynamic infographic or video explaining the system, its connections, and the consequences of disruptions.

Students use Google Maps to identify and analyse real-world systems in their local community, such as transport networks, water flow, or school layouts. They create annotated maps highlighting connections, disruptions, and the impact of change, making abstract systems visible and tangible. This hands-on mapping empowers learners to see 'systems connect us' in action.

Learners design infographics or flowcharts in Canva that visually explain the connections and influences between different human and natural systems (e.g., how the water cycle links to the food system and waste management), focusing on clarity, creativity, and the use of icons and diagrams to communicate complex relationships.

Students create interactive digital books in Book Creator, each page dedicated to a different system (school, natural, technological, social), embedding videos, voice recordings, and diagrams to explain the system's parts, connections, and what happens when it is disrupted. Books can be shared with younger students to foster cross-age learning.

Students explore how systems connect us by building and testing simple digital systems with Micro:bit, mapping how inputs and outputs interact in real time. Through the 'Digital Flashcards' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/digital-flashcards/), learners create interactive flashcards to represent system components, deepening their understanding of how information flows and connects within larger networks.

s.t: Systems in Motion: Rube Goldberg Chain Reaction
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, multi-step Rube Goldberg machine that models a real-world system (such as the water cycle, food chain, or school transport network). Using recycled materials, cardboard, marbles, dominos, and craft supplies, students physically construct each 'stage' of the system, ensuring each part connects and triggers the next. They integrate Sphero Indi robots as moving components?programming them via iPads to activate or respond to certain triggers (e.g., Indi drives onto a colored tile to knock over a lever or start a marble run). The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate system components, plan their chain reactions, prototype and refine their builds, and finally present how their machine demonstrates interconnectedness, change, and disruption within systems.

plannerText: This unit explores the central idea 'Systems connect us' through hands-on investigations of human-made and natural systems, including their parts, connections, and the impact of change or disruption. Students identify, map, and analyse real-world systems in their school and environment, conduct experiments (like the water cycle in a bag), and engage in collaborative projects to deepen their understanding of form, connection, and change within and across systems.

ci: Changes to the Earth impact environment and communities.
lo: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environments cause natural disasters; The impact of natural events on communities

s.d: Transform your students into 'Natural Disaster News Reporters'! In this innovative project, Year 4s will research a real-world natural disaster (such as bushfires, floods, earthquakes, or volcanic eruptions) and create a dynamic multimedia news report using Adobe Express. Students will combine images, video clips (recorded with the iPad Camera app), voiceovers, and text to explain the causes of the disaster, its impact on environments and communities, and how people responded and adapted. This engaging digital storytelling approach empowers students to synthesize their learning, practice digital literacy, and creatively communicate complex scientific ideas to an authentic audience.

s.t: Seesaw App Smash with iPad Camera
s.d: Have students document their hands-on STEM experiments (like the Earthquake Tower or Flood Simulation) using the iPad Camera app. They then upload annotated photos and video reflections to Seesaw, combining visual evidence with voice recordings to explain the science behind the disasters and how communities respond.

Students use Canva to create interactive digital timelines of major Australian natural disasters. They can embed images, maps, and short video clips, highlighting causes, environmental impacts, and community responses. This directly supports the research and case study activities in the unit.

Students simulate natural disasters and monitor environmental changes using Micro:bit sensors, bringing the impact of Earth's changes to life. With the 'Volcano Animations' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/volcano-animations/), learners animate volcanic eruptions and track data, making abstract geological processes tangible and engaging.

s.t: Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss
s.d: Connect the study of floods and community response to real-world engineering by having students use Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/filtering-water-on-the-iss). Students experiment with designing water filtration systems, linking to how communities recover from floods and ensure safe water supplies after disasters.

Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively design, build, and test model communities that can withstand simulated natural disasters. First, they research real-world disasters (earthquakes, floods, bushfires) and brainstorm resilient features. Using physical materials like cardboard, popsticks, recycled plastics, clay, and fabric, they construct miniature buildings, bridges, and landscape features (e.g., levees, retaining walls). Integrating Sphero Indi robots or Bee-Bots, students code the robots to simulate the movement of floodwaters or seismic activity through their models. They observe how their structures respond, document the impact, and iterate their designs for improved resilience. Throughout, students capture their process with iPads (photos/videos), annotate their design choices, and present their final 'resilient community' prototypes to peers, reflecting on how technology and engineering can help communities adapt to Earth's changing environments.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 4 students in exploring how changes to the Earth, such as natural disasters, impact environments and communities. Through hands-on experiments, research projects, digital presentations, and case studies, students investigate the causes of geological change, the effects of extreme environments, and how communities respond and adapt to these challenges.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and kinship; The importance of Country to First Nations people

s.t: Minecraft: Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage
