plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in exploring how storytellers use different methods to convey meaning, with a strong focus on visual, oral, and multimodal storytelling. Activities include author and illustrator investigations, digital story creation, and hands-on experiences that encourage students to experiment with various storytelling techniques and reflect on how stories are constructed and shared.

ci: A sequence of processes enables a system to function.
lo: The systems in our lives; The purpose of various systems; How systems are made of many interdependent parts

s.t: App Smash: Puppet Pals + ChatterPix Kids
s.d: Students design and voice-over their own 'system in action' stories by first creating characters and backgrounds in Puppet Pals, then exporting scenes to ChatterPix Kids to animate and narrate each step of a process (e.g. how lunch orders move from home to the school caf?). This workflow brings abstract systems to life with student voice and creativity, making the invisible visible.

Students document and reflect on real-world systems they encounter at school or home by taking photos or short videos, then annotating with drawings and voice recordings to map out each step in the process. This supports visual thinking and communication of how systems function in their daily lives.

Learners use ScratchJr to visually code simple process-based stories, such as the journey of a letter through Australia Post or the steps in a morning routine. By sequencing blocks, they model interdependent parts and see how changing one step affects the whole system.

Students create visual flowcharts or process maps by arranging photos and stickers in PicCollage to represent the sequence of steps in a chosen system (e.g. recycling, getting ready for school). They can add arrows and labels for clarity, then share with peers for feedback.

Groups design vibrant, interactive posters in Canva that illustrate the components and connections within a familiar system (e.g. the school library's Dewey Decimal system or the evacuation process). They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to layer images and highlight interdependence.

Students work in teams to design, build, and document a physical chain reaction (Rube Goldberg-style) machine that demonstrates how a sequence of processes enables a system to function. Using recycled materials, cardboard, marbles, ramps, levers, and connectors, students create a tangible system where each part triggers the next. They integrate a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot as a moving component that activates or is activated by their machine (e.g., the robot knocks over a domino line, which triggers a ball to roll). Each group maps their system, labels each step, and records a video tour using iPads, explaining how every part interconnects to achieve a final goal (e.g., ringing a bell or raising a flag), highlighting interdependence and the impact of each part. This project merges digital robotics with hands-on building to bring the concept of systems to life.

plannerText: This unit explores how a sequence of processes enables systems to function, with students investigating, mapping, and presenting real-world systems from daily routines to school operations. Learners engage in hands-on activities, collaborative research, and creative presentations to understand interdependence, process, and the impact of each part within a system.

ci: Changes to materials affect how we use and reuse them in daily life.
lo: Understanding material properties; Reuse and recycling through physical change; Sustainably managing materials

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes with the iPad Merge Explorer app to create interactive, 3D visualizations of recycled materials transforming through physical changes (e.g., melting, crushing, reshaping). They record short ChatterPix Kids voice-overs for each stage, then app smash into Book Creator to make a digital 'Journey of a Material' storybook, blending tactile, AR, and creative storytelling.

Students document their hands-on experiments with bending, stretching, and reusing materials by capturing photos and videos in Seesaw. They annotate with voice recordings, explaining how the material changed and how it could be reused or recycled, making their learning visible and accessible for peer feedback.

Learners create vibrant, visual posters in PicCollage showing 'Before and After' states of materials (e.g., a plastic bottle before and after recycling). They use stickers, cut-outs, and their own photos to illustrate the process, then share their posters digitally to inspire sustainable practices in the school community.

Using Delightex, students collaboratively design interactive concept maps that connect material properties, physical changes, and sustainable uses. They drag and drop images, record short audio notes, and visually map out how changes to materials impact their daily lives and the environment.

Students explore the 'Radical Recycling' lesson in Minecraft Education, where they play games and complete challenges to learn about recycling processes and the impact of material reuse. This immersive digital experience reinforces the central idea by letting students experiment with recycling systems in a virtual world.

Students work in teams to design and build imaginative 'material sorting stations' using recycled boxes, tubes, and everyday items. Each station features a physical track or ramp system that channels different types of materials (e.g., paper, plastic, metal, fabric) into sorting zones. Students program a Sphero Indi robot (or similar) with colour-coded paths and attach simple custom bumpers or scoops made from craft materials. The challenge: as the Indi travels along the track, it pushes or shuttles real material samples into the correct sorting zones. Students prototype, test, and refine both their physical stations and the robot's path, using the Design Cycle. The project culminates in a class showcase where groups demonstrate their creative, tech-integrated recycling solutions and explain material properties and sustainability connections.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in hands-on investigations of material properties, physical changes, and sustainable practices. Through experiments, cultural connections, and creative projects, students explore how materials can be reused and recycled, culminating in innovative showcases and real-world action for sustainability.

ci: Patterns in the sky help us understand our place in the universe.
lo: Earth's place in the solar system; Observable patterns in the sky; Tools and methods we use to observe the sky

s.t: App Smash: ChatterPix Kids + Book Creator
s.d: Students create talking planets or moons using ChatterPix Kids, recording their own voices to share facts or stories about observable sky patterns. These animated clips are then combined in Book Creator to produce a collaborative, interactive class eBook about the solar system and patterns in the sky, making learning highly visual, creative, and student-driven.

Students document their daily sky observations by taking photos of the sky at different times and annotating them with drawings or voice recordings. This ongoing digital sky journal helps them track and reflect on patterns such as sunrise, sunset, and moon phases, directly connecting to the unit's focus on observable sky patterns.

Students use ScratchJr on iPads to create simple animations showing the movement of the Earth, sun, and moon, or to model day/night and moon phases. This hands-on coding activity reinforces understanding of cycles and patterns in the sky in a highly visual, age-appropriate way.

Students design vibrant, visual posters about the solar system or sky patterns using PicCollage. They can combine their own photos, stickers, and drawings to create awareness posters or infographics, which can be shared with the school community to teach others about the importance of understanding sky patterns.

Students work in pairs to create digital storyboards or visual presentations in Canva, illustrating how different cultures use sky patterns for calendars, navigation, or storytelling. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to create layered, imaginative scenes without the need for green screen kits.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, tactile model of a planet surface (e.g., Mars or the Moon) using cardboard, papier-m?ch?, sand, pebbles, and recycled materials to create craters, mountains, and valleys. Next, they collaboratively construct and decorate simple 'rovers' using LEGO or recycled parts, attaching markers or flags. Using Sphero Indi robots hidden inside their rovers, students program basic routes (with color tiles or iPad) to navigate their rovers through the physical terrain, simulating an exploration mission. Along the way, students place 'discovery tokens' (handmade artifacts representing things like moon rocks, ice, or alien fossils) that rovers must find and collect. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate real rover missions, plan and sketch their terrain and rover designs, build and test their models, program routes, and reflect on how their prototypes help us learn about the solar system. This hybrid maker experience deepens understanding of planetary features, robotics, and teamwork through imaginative, hands-on exploration.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in exploring patterns in the sky to understand Earth's place in the solar system. Activities include hands-on sky observations, moon diaries, shadow tracking, collaborative solar system models, cultural astronomy investigations, and STEM challenges, all designed to foster inquiry, creativity, and real-world connections to the central idea.

ci: Water is a limited resource that is essential to life.
lo: Unequal access to water; Communities affected by water access; Transferral of water from source to use; Responsibility as global citizens

s.t: App Smash: ChatterPix Kids + Book Creator
s.d: Students photograph water-saving actions or water systems around the school using iPads, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the photos with their own voices explaining why water is essential and how to conserve it. These talking images are then combined in Book Creator to produce a collaborative digital storybook about water as a limited resource, which can be shared with the school community.

Students document their daily water usage at home and school by taking photos or drawing pictures in Seesaw. They can record voice reflections about how their actions impact water conservation, fostering awareness of their personal water footprint.

Learners create vibrant visual posters in PicCollage to advocate for water conservation, using images, stickers, and minimal text. These posters can be displayed digitally around the school or printed for a water awareness campaign.

Using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature, students create before-and-after images showing the effects of water pollution or drought. They can design eye-catching infographics to communicate the importance of clean water and responsible usage.

s.t: Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss
s.d: Students explore how water is filtered and reused on the International Space Station, then experiment with creating their own water filtering system in Minecraft Education. This hands-on digital activity connects directly to the unit's focus on water as a limited and essential resource.

s.t: Water Wise Village: Interactive Water Flow Challenge
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a model village using recycled materials (cardboard, plastic bottles, straws, sponges, etc.), with houses, gardens, and communal spaces. They construct a physical water system?channels, reservoirs, and water towers?to simulate the journey of water from source to homes, highlighting challenges of fair distribution. Using Sphero Indi cars or Bee-Bots, students program routes to 'deliver' water tokens to different areas, navigating obstacles representing real-world issues (e.g., blocked pipes, drought zones). Teams iterate designs through the Design Cycle, testing how their systems conserve water and ensure every part of the village receives enough. Reflection includes digital documentation (photos/videos) of prototypes and sharing strategies for equitable water sharing.

plannerText: This unit explores water as a limited resource essential to life, focusing on unequal access, the impact on communities, the journey of water from source to use, and our responsibility for fair distribution. Students engage in hands-on, creative, and digital activities to investigate water systems, promote conservation, and take action as global citizens.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploring the past helps us to understand the present.
lo: Evidence and its significance in a place; Connections between the past and present within a place; Personal history shapes the present

s.d: Launch an immersive 'Past vs Present' AR museum: students use iPads to photograph Wesley artefacts and campus sites, then app smash with Delightex to create interactive augmented reality scenes. These scenes overlay historical facts, student voice recordings, and digital drawings directly onto real-world locations, letting peers 'step into history' as they explore the campus.

Students embark on a campus walk with Google Maps, dropping digital pins to document artefacts and evidence of Wesley College's history. They annotate each location with photos and reflections, creating a living map that connects past and present. This immersive activity transforms local history into an interactive, student-driven exploration.

Create a collaborative digital timeline: each student authors a page about a significant Wesley artefact, event, or personal family history, combining photos, drawings, and voice narration. The final class eBook visually connects past and present through student perspectives.

Run a 'Wonder Walk' reflection: after the campus history walk, students upload photos and audio reflections to Seesaw, tagging evidence of the past and explaining its significance. Families can comment, adding their own memories or connections.

Design a 'Then & Now' poster series: students use Canva's remove background feature to blend old and new images of Wesley, layering text and icons to highlight changes and continuities. These can be printed or displayed digitally around the school.

s.t: Time Capsule Quest: Interactive Museum in a Box
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a miniature 'museum exhibit' inside a large shoebox or recycled container. Each team selects a significant event or artefact from Wesley College's history, then crafts physical replicas using clay, cardboard, fabric, and found objects. To bring their exhibit to life, they integrate a simple programmable device (e.g., Sphero Indi or Makey Makey): for example, pressing a crafted button or placing a 'visitor' figure in a certain spot triggers a light, sound, or movement related to their artefact's story. Students use the Design Cycle: they research, plan, prototype, test, and improve their interactive museums. The final exhibits are displayed as a 'Museum Walk', where peers and families can explore and activate each interactive time capsule, experiencing Wesley's past through hands-on discovery.

plannerText: Students investigate the significance of evidence and artefacts at Wesley College, making connections between past and present through campus walks, guest speakers, excursions, and interviews. They document and reflect on changes over time, culminating in creative projects that showcase how personal and community history shapes the present.

ci: Humans have a responsibility in their relationships to value the rights of others
lo: The difference between needs and wants; Everybody has rights and responsibilities; What is a respectful relationship

s.d: Create an interactive digital 'Respect Quilt' using Delightex, where each student designs a visual square representing a right or responsibility, then app smash with ChatterPix Kids to animate their square and record their voice sharing how they value others' rights. Combine all into a class quilt to display on the IWB, fostering both creativity and student voice.

Students document real-life scenarios of respectful and disrespectful relationships by drawing, photographing, or acting them out using the iPad camera. They then upload and annotate these on Seesaw, reflecting on how their actions can show respect and value the rights of others.

Collaboratively author a digital class book titled 'Our Rights and Responsibilities', where each student contributes a page with drawings and audio explaining a right, responsibility, or act of kindness. The book can be shared with families to promote discussion at home.

Students create vibrant visual collages showing the difference between needs and wants, or respectful vs. disrespectful actions, using photos, stickers, and drawings. These can be printed or shared digitally to reinforce concepts visually.

Students script and record short puppet shows that model conflict resolution and respectful relationships. They use the app's characters and backgrounds to act out scenarios, then share their videos with the class for discussion and peer feedback.

Students collaboratively design and build a giant, 3D boardgame that teaches about needs, wants, rights, and respectful relationships. Using large cardboard sheets, recycled materials, and craft supplies, they construct the game board with physical spaces, challenge zones, and creative obstacles (e.g., bridges, tunnels, pop-up cards). Along the path, students create scenario cards and characters representing diverse backgrounds using clay and craft materials. Integrating Sphero Indi cars, students program routes and color codes so the robot 'player' travels the board, triggering audio messages or actions (recorded via iPads) at key spaces (e.g., 'Share a kind act', 'Solve a needs vs. wants challenge'). The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize by sharing stories, define respectful behaviors, ideate board layouts and challenges, prototype with materials, test robot navigation, and improve based on feedback. The final boardgame becomes an interactive learning tool for the class, reinforcing empathy, respect, and responsibility through hands-on play and digital-physical integration.

plannerText: This unit explores the difference between needs and wants, the importance of rights and responsibilities, and what makes a respectful relationship. Students engage in role-plays, collaborative murals, and reflective discussions to build empathy, kindness, and respect for diversity, while connecting their learning to classroom agreements and real-world scenarios.

ci: Creativity allows people to express their understanding of the world.
lo: The ways people use technology to express creativity; How texts and images work together; How technology influences our creativity

s.d: Launch a collaborative 'Imagination Portal' project where students use Delightex to co-design fantastical worlds, then 'app smash' by exporting their creations into Book Creator to write and illustrate interactive stories that express their unique perspectives and creative processes.

Students design digital posters or zines in Canva that showcase how technology and creativity intersect in their lives, using a mix of text, images, and visual storytelling to communicate their personal understanding of creativity.

Facilitate a stop motion animation challenge where students storyboard, create, and film short animations using Stop Motion Studio, then enhance their projects with soundtracks and effects in iMovie to express a message or story about creativity.

Students bring their creative ideas to life by designing interactive 3D scenes or digital sculptures using Merge Cubes and iPads. They showcase their understanding of how creativity expresses our view of the world by building augmented reality models inspired by contemporary artists, then record video walkthroughs to share their process and reflections. This immersive approach lets students literally hold their creative thinking in their hands, making abstract ideas tangible and exciting.

Set up a class Padlet as a digital 'Creativity Gallery' where students regularly upload photos, sketches, or audio clips of their creative process, and comment on each other's work to foster a community of creative expression and feedback.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and build a kinetic sculpture (automaton) that visually expresses a chosen emotion or idea. First, they brainstorm feelings or concepts they wish to communicate (e.g., joy, curiosity, surprise). Using simple mechanisms (cams, levers, pulleys) crafted from cardboard, recycled materials, and wooden sticks, students physically prototype moving parts that symbolize their chosen expression. Next, they integrate a Sphero Indi or micro:bit as the 'heart' of their automaton, coding it to trigger lights, sounds, or movement in sync with the sculpture's actions. The final creations are displayed in a gallery, where students explain their design choices and how the blend of technology and handcrafting helped them communicate their ideas in a tangible, creative way.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 3 students in exploring how creativity allows people to express their understanding of the world, with a strong focus on using technology for creative expression. Key activities include stop motion animation, graphic novel creation in Book Creator, digital art inspired by contemporary artists, and collaborative workshops that blend visual, audio, and written forms to communicate ideas. Students reflect on their creative process and share their work with peers and parents.

ci: Needs can be met with digital systems.
lo: What are digital systems; Ideas and information can be organised and shared in safe online environments; Being a responsible digital citizen

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of digital systems (like computers, tablets, or networks) using Merge Cube, then create an augmented reality 'Digital Systems Museum.' They document each system's function and responsible use in Book Creator, allowing peers to explore and learn from each other's virtual exhibits.

Students collaboratively build a 'Digital Footprint Wall' on Padlet, uploading icons, drawings, and screenshots of their digital activities. They use Canva to design infographics about safe online environments and digital citizenship, then embed these visuals into the Padlet for a dynamic, interactive class resource.

Students maintain a digital log of their technology use (types of devices, time spent, and activities) in Seesaw, using photos, voice notes, and drawings. They reflect on their digital habits and set personal goals for responsible and balanced technology use, sharing progress with peers for feedback.

Students become digital detectives in 'Minecraft: The Investigators' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-investigators), exploring how information is shared and evaluating the reliability and safety of digital sources. They practice responsible decision-making in simulated online environments, directly linking to digital citizenship and safe information sharing.

Students build and code Micro:bit-powered digital systems to reinforce their understanding of how needs can be met with technology. The 'Computing Fundamentals' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/computing-fundamentals/) guides learners to create simple devices or apps that organise and share information, deepening their digital citizenship and systems thinking.

s.t: Digital Citizenship Playground: Interactive Safety Quest
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a tabletop 'Digital Citizenship Playground'?a physical board or 3D landscape made from cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. The playground features zones representing different aspects of digital citizenship (e.g., Safe Sharing Slide, Privacy Tunnel, Cyberbully Bridge). Students craft obstacles, signs, and interactive elements for each zone. Using Sphero Indi robots decorated as 'Digital Citizens,' students program movement paths to navigate the playground, triggering physical challenges and scenario cards at each stop (e.g., build a popstick barrier for a privacy challenge or design a warning sign for unsafe content). Teams iterate through the Design Cycle: empathise (identify digital dilemmas), define (choose playground features), ideate (sketch and plan zones), prototype (build and decorate), test (navigate with Indi and role-play challenges), and reflect (share solutions and improvements). The result is an engaging, hands-on journey that blends robotics, creative building, and real-world digital citizenship scenarios.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore what digital systems are, how ideas and information can be organised and shared in safe online environments, and what it means to be a responsible digital citizen. Activities include technology timelines, digital logs, cyber safety projects, digital footprint art, and the creation of individual Essential Agreements for digital citizenship.

ci: Observing how materials change helps us make sense of the physical world.
lo: Observable properties of solids and liquids; How heating and cooling change state; Scientific thinking helps solve everyday problems

s.d: Students use Merge Cubes with the iPad Merge Explorer app to create interactive, augmented reality models showing how different materials change state (e.g., melting, freezing, dissolving). They record short video explanations using the iPad camera, then app smash into Book Creator to build a digital 'States of Matter Museum' with AR experiences and student voiceovers.

Launch a collaborative Padlet where students upload photos and videos of real-life examples of materials changing state at home or school (e.g., melting ice, butter, chocolate). They annotate their posts with scientific vocabulary and explanations, building a living wall of evidence for class discussion.

Students design visually engaging infographics in Canva to explain the process of heating and cooling materials, using diagrams, icons, and step-by-step visuals. They can include QR codes linking to their own video demonstrations or experiments.

s.t: Minecraft: Filtering Water On The Iss
s.d: Students explore how water is filtered on the International Space Station by experimenting with creating a water filtering system in Minecraft Education. This directly links to understanding how materials can change state and be separated or purified through physical processes.

Students use Micro:bit to conduct experiments on changes of state, collecting temperature or timing data as they melt, freeze, or heat materials. The 'Data Handling' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/data-handling/) enables learners to visualise and analyse their findings, making scientific inquiry hands-on and data-driven.

Students work in teams to design and build interactive arcade games that demonstrate changes of state (solid, liquid, gas) through heating and cooling. Using recycled cardboard, plastic bottles, LEGO, and craft materials, each group constructs a physical game (e.g., a marble run where marbles represent particles, or a claw machine that 'melts' or 'freezes' objects). To integrate technology, students embed simple circuits with Makey Makey or microcontroller kits and connect their games to Scratch on iPads or laptops. When players trigger certain actions (like moving a lever or pressing a button), sensors activate digital animations or sounds showing the scientific process (e.g., melting chocolate, freezing water). The project follows the Design Cycle: students brainstorm, prototype, test, and refine their arcade games, then host a 'Thermal Arcade Day' where peers play, learn, and give feedback. All reflections and scientific explanations are captured in short student-made video walkthroughs using iPads.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in hands-on investigations of solids and liquids, exploring how heating and cooling cause changes of state. Students classify materials, conduct experiments (e.g., melting chocolate, freezing water), and use scientific thinking to solve real-life problems, documenting their learning through collaborative digital platforms and reflective activities.

ci: The diversity of soils, rocks, and minerals shapes the way people value and use Earth's resources.
lo: Observable properties of soils, rocks and minerals; Earth's resources are vital and must be used responsibly; First Nations knowledge of soils and rocks

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of different soil, rock, and mineral types using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They record short video explanations using the iPad camera, then use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to overlay themselves as 'virtual geologists' presenting their findings. This immersive, hands-on approach brings earth science to life and allows students to 'hold' and explore their digital creations in augmented reality.

Launch a collaborative global 'Soil, Rock, and Mineral Museum' on Padlet, where students upload photos and research about samples collected from their homes or school grounds. They can compare findings, comment on each other's posts, and map out where each sample was found, fostering a sense of global connection and resource diversity.

Students author digital field journals in Book Creator, documenting their soil and rock investigations with photos, diagrams, and voice recordings. They can include interviews with family members about traditional uses of earth materials, integrating First Nations perspectives and personal stories.

Design visually striking infographics in Canva that explain the journey of a resource (e.g., from mineral to everyday object), highlighting responsible use and sustainability. Students can use Canva's 'Remove Background' tool to create layered scenes showing the impact of resource extraction and conservation.

Students explore the Minecraft: Radical Recycling lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/radical-recycling) to understand recycling processes and the importance of using Earth's resources wisely. They participate in in-game challenges that reinforce the value of sustainable practices and resource management.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, multi-layered 3D diorama that showcases the diversity of local soils, rocks, and minerals, integrating both scientific and cultural perspectives (including First Nations knowledge). Using physical materials like clay, sand, rocks, recycled cardboard, and natural found objects, they craft realistic landscapes with visible soil profiles and embedded 'resource zones.' Students then code simple interactive elements using Sphero Indi cars or Bee-Bots: they create a quest path, placing color-coded markers or sensors in different soil/mineral zones. As the robot navigates the diorama, it triggers audio stories, facts, or digital artwork (recorded or created by students on iPads) about each resource's significance, responsible use, and cultural value. The result is a hands-on, tech-enhanced exhibit that fuses digital storytelling, robotics, and physical making to deepen understanding of Earth's resources and diverse perspectives.

plannerText: This unit explores how the diversity of soils, rocks, and minerals shapes the way people value and use Earth's resources. Students investigate observable properties, responsible use, and cultural perspectives?including First Nations knowledge?through hands-on science, comparative research, and creative digital projects.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Land and place are represented and valued in different ways.
lo: How Australia and neighbouring regions define places; Australia's states/territories have unique identities; First Nations connections to Country

s.d: Students create interactive 3D 'place cubes' using Merge Cubes to represent different Australian states, First Nations Countries, or neighbouring regions. Each face of the cube features student-created digital artefacts (photos, drawings, audio reflections) about how land and place are valued. Students then app smash by embedding their Merge Cube experiences into a Book Creator digital journal, adding reflections and poetry inspired by their explorations.

Students design visually rich digital posters or infographics in Canva that compare how land and place are represented in First Nations Australian perspectives versus contemporary state/territory boundaries. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to layer images, maps, and student artwork, making cultural and historical perspectives visible.

Students use Google Maps to explore representations of land and place, comparing First Nations Countries, Australian states, and neighbouring regions. They create custom maps highlighting cultural, historical, and geographical features, deepening their understanding of how land is valued and represented in different ways. This digital mapping fosters empathy and perspective-taking.

Using 'Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions' (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions), students research and build digital representations of important inventions or cultural artefacts from different Australian regions or neighbouring countries. They reflect on how these innovations connect to place, identity, and value.

Students create short, dynamic video stories in Adobe Express, weaving together poetry, images, and narration to express their personal or cultural connection to a chosen place. They can highlight how perspectives on land and place differ across Australia and its neighbours.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, walkable floor map of Australia and its neighbouring regions using cardboard, fabric, recycled materials, and natural elements (leaves, sticks, sand). On each state, territory, and First Nations Country, students craft physical landmarks, cultural icons, and mini-sculptures from clay or recycled items. Using iPads, students record short audio stories, poems, or interviews reflecting the significance of each place, especially First Nations perspectives. They then attach QR codes or NFC tags to their physical creations; when scanned, these trigger the digital stories. The entire map becomes an interactive, hands-on journey: as classmates walk the path, they physically engage with the crafted landscape and digitally experience layered narratives, deepening their understanding of how land and place are valued from multiple viewpoints. This project follows the Design Cycle: students inquire into different places, ideate and prototype map features, create both physical and digital components, test their interactive path with peers, and reflect on their learning and design choices.

plannerText: This unit explores how land and place are represented and valued in different ways, focusing on Australia's states, territories, First Nations Countries, and neighbouring regions. Students investigate cultural, historical, and geographical perspectives through mapping, poetry, research, and creative digital projects, with a strong emphasis on First Nations connections to Country and diverse ways of understanding place.

ci: The choices and actions we make contribute to our health and wellbeing.
lo: What it means to be healthy; Choices and their impact on health; Actions to maintain a healthy mind and body

s.d: Students create an interactive 'Choose Your Own Adventure' scenario about daily health choices using Delightex, then use Canva to visually enhance each decision point with infographics and illustrations. This empowers students to explore the real-life consequences of their choices on health and wellbeing in a dynamic, gamified way.

Students document their personal health journeys by creating digital journals in Book Creator, combining photos, voice recordings, and reflections on their food, exercise, sleep, and mindfulness routines. This allows for multimodal self-assessment and sharing with families via Seesaw.

Learners use Micro:bit to track and reflect on their health and wellbeing choices, programming devices to log steps, sleep, or mindfulness activities. The 'Being Active' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/being-active/) empowers students to set personal goals and visualise progress, making healthy habits tangible and motivating.

Students design and distribute a wellbeing survey to classmates and families, collecting data on sleep, nutrition, and activity habits. They then analyse the results in class, using the findings to spark discussions about trends, challenges, and opportunities for healthier choices.

Students create vibrant digital posters or short video PSAs in Adobe Express, advocating for a specific healthy habit (e.g., 'Drink More Water!' or 'Screen-Free Bedtime'). These can be shared on Seesaw or displayed around the school to inspire action.

s.t: Wellbeing Wearables: Design Your Own Healthy Habits Tracker
s.d: Students work through the Design Cycle to invent and prototype a wearable device (such as a wristband or badge) that helps them make healthy choices each day. Using recycled materials, felt, cardboard, and craft supplies, students physically build the wearable, then integrate a simple digital component?such as a programmable LED badge (e.g., using a basic Makey Makey, Circuit Playground Express, or similar age-appropriate microcontroller)?that lights up or changes color to track actions like drinking water, moving, or practicing mindfulness. Students brainstorm features, sketch designs, build and decorate their wearables, then program the device to respond to simple touch or button inputs representing healthy habits. The project culminates in a 'Wellbeing Expo' where students demonstrate how their creations encourage positive daily choices.

plannerText: This unit explores how the choices and actions students make contribute to their health and wellbeing. Activities include investigating nutrition, exercise, sleep, and mindfulness, engaging with guest speakers, analysing data, and creating personal health action plans. Students reflect on their learning and take action to improve their own wellbeing.

ci: Responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking.
lo: Ways forms of expression convey messages; Creative and critical thinking skills; The language used to respond to The Arts

s.d: Launch a collaborative 'Virtual Art Gallery of the Imagination' using Delightex for students to design immersive, interactive 3D art installations inspired by their poetry, music, or visual art. Students then embed their creations into a Padlet, where peers can 'walk through' and leave reflective comments, fostering a digital curation experience that mirrors a real-world art show.

Students create digital artist statements and visual reflections using Canva, combining images of their own artwork, poetry, and excursion photos with layered text and design elements to communicate the mood, message, and inspiration behind their creative pieces.

App smash Book Creator with the iPad camera to produce multimedia poetry anthologies. Students record themselves reading their poems, add photos from the NGV excursion, and annotate artworks with their interpretations, creating a rich, interactive eBook for sharing at the art show.

Facilitate a 'Lyric & Art Mashup' project where students use Adobe Express to design animated lyric videos. They select a song (e.g., from the Bruno Mars lyric analysis), illustrate key lines with digital drawings, and animate transitions to visually express the song's mood and meaning.

Host a 'Response Wall' on Seesaw where students upload short video reflections after each art experience (excursion, incursion, or class activity), responding to prompts about how the artwork made them think or feel, and giving peer feedback to deepen collective understanding.

Students explore self-expression by designing and building interactive self-portraits that respond to touch or movement. Using Makey Makey kits (or micro:bit touch sensors), students craft physical portraits from recycled cardboard, fabric, yarn, and mixed media. They embed conductive materials (aluminum foil, copper tape) into their art, connecting facial features or accessories. When touched, these trigger digital soundscapes, short poems, or light patterns (programmed on Scratch or micro:bit) that express different emotions or moods. Students follow the Design Cycle: empathise (explore feelings and artistic styles), define (choose emotions to express), ideate (sketch interactive features), prototype (build and wire up the portraits), test (share and refine), and reflect. The final gallery lets visitors interact with each portrait, experiencing the creator's unique artistic and emotional expression through both physical art and digital response.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in diverse artistic expressions, including poetry, visual art inspired by Yayoi Kusama, music composition, and excursions to the NGV and State Theatre. Students analyse, create, and respond to art forms, culminating in a student-led art show where they display and reflect on their own creative works.

ci: Communities create rules, laws and services to help people live together fairly and safely.
lo: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that help leaders guide others; How leadership impacts communities

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of community spaces (like a council chamber or emergency services hub) using Merge Cubes, then create an augmented reality 'choose your own adventure' story in Book Creator where users make decisions as community leaders, exploring the impact of different rules and services on fairness and safety.

Students collaboratively design and distribute a digital survey to gather data from peers and families about which community rules and services are most valued, then analyse the results to inform a class debate on effective community organisation.

Create a digital 'Community Noticeboard' where students post and respond to real-world scenarios involving rules, laws, and services, encouraging peer-to-peer discussion and reflection on fairness and safety in communities.

Students work in teams to design persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for a new community service or law, using Canva's design tools and 'Remove Background' feature to create visually compelling arguments.

Students explore the stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and build their own virtual community in Minecraft Education, making decisions about rules, laws, and services to ensure fairness and safety for all citizens.

s.t: Justice in Action: Community Courtroom Makerspace
s.d: Students work in teams to design, build, and role-play a miniature courtroom and town square using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each team creates physical models of key community buildings (court, council, police station, etc.), then codes a Sphero Indi or Bee-Bot as the 'citizen' navigating real-life rule or law scenarios (e.g., resolving a dispute, reporting a problem). Teams use iPads to document their process and record mock 'court cases' where they debate and enact fair solutions, integrating digital storytelling with hands-on construction and collaborative problem-solving.

plannerText: This unit explores how communities create rules, laws, and services to help people live together fairly and safely. Students investigate effective leadership, strengths, and actions, engage in scenarios and debates about community organisation, and take action through persuasive speeches and collaborative projects.

ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of their animal enrichment prototypes using Merge Cubes, then document the design process, material choices, and environmental impact in a digital science journal on Book Creator. This app smash allows students to virtually 'hold' and explore their creations while reflecting on sustainable design choices.

s.t: Minecraft: Its Good To Be Green
s.d: Students explore a sustainable building in Minecraft Education, analyzing how material choices and design decisions impact the environment. They can then reflect on how these principles could be applied to their own enrichment toy prototypes.

Students create visually engaging infographics or posters in Canva to communicate the life cycle of materials used in their prototypes, highlighting which are biodegradable, recyclable, or reusable, and the impact these choices have on the natural world.

Students program Sphero BOLT robots to simulate animal movement and interaction with different enrichment toy prototypes. This hands-on coding activity helps them test and iterate their designs for functionality and animal engagement.

Students design and test sustainable product prototypes using Micro:bit sensors to measure environmental impact or product performance. The 'Energy Awareness' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/energy-awareness/) guides learners to monitor energy use and consider eco-friendly design, connecting STEM innovation to real-world sustainability challenges.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and build interactive animal enrichment games inspired by arcade machines, focusing on sustainability and animal wellbeing. Working in teams, students brainstorm animal needs, sketch ideas, and prototype enrichment devices using recycled cardboard, plastic bottles, fabric, string, and natural materials. They integrate programmable Bee-Bots or Sphero Indi robots as 'moving parts'?for example, a Bee-Bot could trigger a treat dispenser or reveal a hidden toy when an animal interacts with the device. Teams test and refine their creations, documenting iterations in their digital science journals, and reflect on how their material choices impact both function and environmental footprint.

plannerText: Students investigate how material properties influence product design, research sustainable solutions, and document their learning in digital science journals. They experiment with different materials, compare animal enrichment toys, and ultimately design and prototype their own enrichment device for animals, considering both functionality and environmental impact.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: Diversity of First Nations people; Importance of community, family and traditions; Importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students create interactive Dreamtime stories or virtual dioramas using Merge Cubes, overlaying 3D symbols, animals, and landscapes significant to First Nations cultures. They then app smash by documenting their creative process and cultural reflections in Book Creator, combining AR visuals, text, and audio explanations to deepen understanding of Country, language, and community.

Design a collaborative digital mural in Canva where each student contributes a panel representing an aspect of First Nations identity, such as language, Country, or community. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to incorporate student photos or artwork, and layer in authentic Indigenous symbols and patterns for a visually rich, respectful mural.

Build a living digital map on Padlet where students pin locations of different First Nations groups, add audio snippets of local languages, and share research on traditions, family structures, and connections to Country. Encourage students to comment and ask questions to foster inquiry and respectful dialogue.

Students record video reflections or interviews with family members about their own connections to place, language, and community, then compare and contrast with First Nations perspectives. Use Seesaw to curate a class gallery of these stories, highlighting diversity and shared values.

Students explore how First Nations peoples' knowledge of Country supports biodiversity by building 'beetopias' in Minecraft Education. They apply Indigenous perspectives on land stewardship and sustainable habitats, then reflect on the importance of community and tradition in environmental care.

Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, winding river diorama using recycled materials, clay, cardboard, natural objects, and paint, representing the importance of waterways in First Nations cultures. Along the river, students craft significant landmarks, flora, and fauna, inspired by Dreamtime stories and Indigenous art. Integrating Sphero Indi robots, students code cultural storytelling 'journeys'?the robots follow hand-crafted, painted paths along the river, stopping at key points where QR codes or NFC tags (embedded in crafted rocks or trees) trigger audio narrations, student-created digital artworks, or Dreamtime story segments on iPads. The project blends physical making, coding, and digital storytelling, deepening understanding of Indigenous connections to Country and water.

plannerText: Students explore the diversity, traditions, and connections of First Nations people to Country, language, and community through Dreamtime stories, art, excursions, and hands-on experiences. They create narratives, dioramas, and digital projects to synthesise their learning about Indigenous perspectives and cultural significance.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.
lo: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; Consequences of exploration

s.d: Students create interactive 3D exploration artefacts using Merge Cubes, embedding digital content (audio, video, text) about famous explorers or key discoveries. They then app smash by documenting their process and reflections in a multimedia Book Creator journal, allowing peers to 'hold' and explore their artefacts virtually.

Students explore the Minecraft Hall of Inventions world, investigating key innovations from history. They then design and build their own 'invention' inspired by a historical explorer or era, reflecting on how exploration led to new opportunities and understandings. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions

Learners use Google Maps to follow the journeys of famous explorers, plotting routes, marking discoveries, and annotating key moments along the way. They compare historical maps with modern satellite imagery, sparking discussions about the consequences and opportunities of exploration. This interactive mapping brings the excitement and complexity of discovery to life.

Classes collaborate on a live Padlet 'Exploration Wall', where students post research, questions, and multimedia findings about different explorers, mapping connections between historical events, motivations, and outcomes in real time.

Learners use Micro:bit to map explorers' journeys and simulate the challenges of exploration, coding devices to represent discoveries and obstacles along the way. The 'Data Handling' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/data-handling/) enables students to visualise and analyse exploration data, connecting historical inquiry with digital innovation.

Students work in teams to design and build a 'Time Traveler's Exploration Pod'?a creative, transportable model vehicle inspired by both historical and futuristic exploration methods. Using cardboard, recycled materials, wheels, fabric, and craft supplies, students prototype their pod to represent a chosen era or style of exploration (e.g., ancient sea voyages, space travel, desert caravans). They then integrate programmable Sphero Indi robots as the 'engine' of their pods, coding routes that represent a famous explorer's journey mapped out on a large collaborative floor map. The pods must physically carry a small 'artifact' (clay or 3D-printed token) from one destination to another, simulating the movement and challenges of real explorers. Throughout the Design Cycle, students ideate, sketch, prototype, test, and refine both their vehicle and code, reflecting on how exploration tools have evolved and the obstacles faced across time.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in the concept of exploration, focusing on why people explore, how exploration has changed over time, and the consequences of these journeys. Students investigate historical and modern explorers, engage in mapping and narrative writing, and present their findings through a variety of digital and creative formats.

ci: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.
lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

s.d: Students create interactive 3D Merge Cube experiences representing different places of worship or symbols from world religions. They then app smash by embedding videos, audio reflections, and AR screenshots into a digital Book Creator 'Belief Museum', allowing students to virtually 'hold' and explore diverse beliefs in their hands.

Launch a global 'Belief Wall' where students post digital postcards about their own beliefs, family traditions, or research on world religions. Encourage multimedia posts (audio, video, images) and invite respectful questions and comments to foster intercultural dialogue and empathy.

Students design visually stunning infographics comparing the core beliefs, symbols, and practices of major world religions. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create digital collages of places of worship, and present findings in a class 'Belief Gallery Walk'.

Curate a playlist of short documentaries and virtual tours of places of worship from Clickview. Students use the platform's interactive video features to annotate, ask questions, and reflect on similarities and differences between spiritual practices.

Students record video interviews with family members or community leaders about their beliefs and traditions using the iPad camera. They upload and annotate these interviews in Seesaw, tagging key vocabulary and reflecting on how beliefs shape identity.

Students work in teams to research different world religions and spiritual beliefs, identifying key symbols, artefacts, and values. Using recycled materials (cardboard, fabric, string, clay, etc.), each group physically builds a symbolic 'island' representing a belief system?incorporating meaningful structures, colours, and objects. Next, teams collaboratively design and construct 'bridges' (using craft sticks, LEGO, or connectors) that link the islands, symbolizing shared values or respectful connections between beliefs. The bridges feature simple programmable elements: students embed Sphero Indi cars or similar robots, coding them with colour tiles to travel across the bridges and stop at key points where students record short explanations (using iPads to trigger audio via QR codes or NFC tags attached to the bridges). This hands-on project blends physical creativity, digital storytelling, and robotics, helping students visualize and celebrate the diversity and interconnectedness of human beliefs.

plannerText: This unit explores how people's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life, focusing on spiritual and religious beliefs, similarities and differences among religions, and the impact of these beliefs on human diversity. Students engage in gallery walks, virtual tours, research, and creative projects to deepen understanding and foster respect for different worldviews.

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

s.d: Students design and prototype interactive 3D models of disaster-resistant shelters or emergency tools using Tinkercad, then bring their creations to life with Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record AR video walkthroughs using the iPad camera, narrating their design thinking and how their solution meets a specific community need. This immersive, hands-on workflow makes abstract design concepts tangible and engaging.

Students create persuasive digital pitch decks for their UNICEF-inspired design solutions, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to showcase their prototypes and integrate infographics, maps, and empathy maps. This supports the pitch-writing and presentation phase, allowing students to visually communicate their process and impact.

Throughout the inquiry, students collaboratively document their ideation, research, and empathy mapping on a shared Padlet board. They can upload photos of their prototypes, maps, and annotated infographics, creating a living portfolio that tracks their design journey and peer feedback.

Teams publish interactive digital journals in Book Creator, combining photos, videos, voice recordings, and diagrams to document each stage of the design thinking process. This supports reflection, self-assessment, and sharing with the wider school community.

Students prototype and test their natural hazard solutions using Micro:bit sensors to collect real-time data on environmental conditions. The 'Energy Awareness' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/energy-awareness/) empowers learners to monitor and optimise their designs, making the design thinking process more authentic and evidence-based.

For the STEM Design Cycle project, students use Lego Spike Prime to engineer and prototype a disaster-resilient structure or emergency response robot. They apply the full design thinking cycle: empathising with stakeholders, ideating, prototyping, testing for stability or function (e.g., earthquake shake table or flood simulation), and refining based on feedback. This hands-on challenge directly links to the unit's focus on purposeful design for community needs.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore how people design for purpose through the lens of natural hazards and community needs. They engage in the design thinking process, research real-world problems, collaborate in teams, prototype innovative solutions, and pitch their ideas to an authentic audience, integrating empathy, data analysis, and persuasive communication throughout.

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: Students design and build interactive 3D models of their 'ideal community' or economic system using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They then use the iPad camera to record a virtual guided tour, narrating how wealth, resources, and responsibilities are distributed in their imagined society. This merges tactile, spatial, and digital storytelling for a futuristic approach to economic systems.

Launch a collaborative 'Wealth is More Than Money' digital wall where students post multimedia reflections (audio, drawings, photos of their Pop Art, or short videos) exploring non-monetary forms of wealth in their lives and communities. Students then use Seesaw to create digital portfolios tracking their evolving understanding of economic systems and personal values.

Students produce an 'Economic Voices' podcast, simulating interviews with historical and contemporary figures about economic systems, wealth, and responsibilities. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that bring economic concepts to life through storytelling and debate.

Students explore the evolution of economic systems by visiting the virtual museum in Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions. They research a key economic innovation (e.g., currency, banking, trade networks), then build their own invention in the museum, explaining its impact on society and connecting it to their classroom inquiry. This fosters historical perspective and creative application.

Students use Excel to model personal or community budgets, experimenting with different income sources, expenses, and savings strategies. They create interactive spreadsheets that visually demonstrate the consequences of financial decisions, supporting critical thinking about economic responsibility and resource allocation.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype a 'Smart Marketplace' using Lego Spike Prime. They design and code automated systems (e.g., a robotic cashier, inventory tracker, or sustainable delivery vehicle) that address real-world economic challenges such as fair trade, accessibility, or environmental impact. Teams document their process and pitch their solution to the class, integrating technology, economics, and design thinking.

plannerText: Students inquire into economic systems by exploring wealth beyond money, responsibilities in managing income, and diverse ways income is earned and managed. They engage in provocations, debates, budgeting simulations, job applications, and collaborative projects to design ideal communities, culminating in creative presentations and hands-on design challenges that connect economic concepts to real-world contexts.

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of natural disasters (e.g., bushfires, earthquakes, floods) using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app, then record AR video explanations using the iPad camera. They can app smash with Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to place themselves inside their AR scenes, narrating the science and human impact for a truly immersive digital artifact.

Students use Minecraft Education's 'Invisible Forces At Work' lesson to explore magnetism and gravity, then design a magnetic structure that could protect the International Space Station or assist astronauts. This directly connects to the unit's focus on the interactions between materials and forces in the real world.

Students document their design thinking journey by creating multimedia digital books that combine photos of prototypes, data graphs, scientific explanations, and personal reflections on how their solutions address real-world problems caused by natural disasters.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'Wonder Wall' on Padlet, posting questions, research findings, and video clips from Clickview about natural hazards, then use Padlet's mapping feature to visually track disaster locations and impacts across Australia.

Students use Micro:bit to simulate and monitor the effects of natural disasters, collecting and analysing data to inform their disaster response solutions. The 'Volcano Animations' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/volcano-animations/) brings the science of bushfires, earthquakes, and floods to life, making risk analysis and STEM problem-solving hands-on and engaging.

Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to prototype and test earthquake-resistant buildings with Lego Spike Prime. They iterate on their designs based on data from simulated 'shake table' tests, documenting their process and results in a digital engineering journal.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in the science of natural disasters, focusing on bushfires, earthquakes, and floods. Through collaborative inquiry, students research hazards, analyse data, map risks, and use the design thinking process to prototype and pitch solutions that protect communities and uphold children's rights. The unit integrates maths, science, geography, and digital technologies, culminating in student-led presentations and reflections.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of local flora and fauna using the Merge Cube and iPads, then use the Merge Explorer app to overlay digital information about conservation needs and stewardship actions. They can record video tours of their models using the iPad camera, narrating how their community can interact with and protect these species.

Students collaboratively research and document local council sustainability initiatives on Padlet, then design persuasive digital posters in Canva to promote their chosen initiative to the school community, integrating real data and student-generated visuals.

After a nature walk around the school, students use Book Creator to compile a multimedia field guide of native plants and animals, including photos, audio reflections, and conservation tips tailored to their local environment.

Students explore how bees interact with their environment by applying their knowledge of stewardship and conservation to design and build digital 'beetopias' in Minecraft, supporting thriving bee colonies and reflecting on the importance of biodiversity.

Learners use Micro:bit to monitor local environmental data as they investigate community interactions with the environment, collecting information on temperature, light, or soil moisture. The 'Healthy Oceans' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/healthy-oceans/) inspires students to design digital solutions for conservation and stewardship, connecting action projects to real-world data and advocacy.

s.t: Lego Spike Prime Design Cycle: Eco-Innovation Challenge
s.d: Using the Design Cycle, students identify a local environmental issue (e.g., waste, water use, habitat loss), brainstorm solutions, and prototype an eco-innovation using Lego Spike Prime. They test, refine, and present their models, justifying design choices based on community values and conservation needs.

plannerText: Students inquire into how communities interact with the environment, focusing on conservation, stewardship, and the influence of community values on environmental management. Key activities include exploring First Nations perspectives, researching local flora and fauna, investigating council initiatives, and pitching persuasive sustainability actions for the school community.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students create interactive 3D artefacts representing key resources discovered in Australia (e.g., gold, wheat, wool) using Merge Cube. They then app smash by embedding their 3D models and explanatory videos into a digital Book Creator journal, allowing for immersive storytelling about how each resource shaped the nation's growth.

Launch a collaborative migration map: students research and post digital 'story cards' on a Padlet world map, each card representing a migrant group or individual who contributed to Australia's development, including their reasons for migration and the impact on society.

Learners create a Gold Rush podcast series, narrating stories from the perspectives of migrants, Indigenous Australians, and colonial settlers. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), students blend research, interviews, and dramatic storytelling to explore how resource discovery shaped nations and changed lives.

Students explore the Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions world, then select a historical Australian invention or resource. They research its significance and build a new exhibit in Minecraft, presenting how this resource or invention contributed to national growth.

Students take on the role of a historical figure (e.g., a gold miner, First Nations Australian, or migrant) and record a series of reflective video journal entries in Seesaw, documenting their experiences and perspectives during key events like the Gold Rush or migration waves.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype a 'Resource Extraction Machine' with Lego Spike Prime, simulating how technology changed resource discovery and extraction in Australia. They iterate designs to maximize efficiency and minimize environmental impact, documenting their process in a digital engineering journal.

plannerText: This unit explores how the discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations, focusing on Australia's Gold Rush, migration, and the effects on Indigenous and colonial populations. Students investigate historical events, analyze cause and effect, research case studies, and create timelines, infographics, and perspective-based narratives to deepen their understanding of economic, social, and environmental change.

ci: Managing change requires acceptance, knowledge and strategies.
lo: Establishing, managing and maintaining relationships; Managing behaviours in digital situations; Managing changes associated with puberty

s.t: Merge Cubes + iPads (Augmented Reality Self-Portrait Gallery)
s.d: Students create digital self-portraits using iPads, then use Merge Cubes to transform their artwork into interactive 3D AR experiences. Each cube face can represent a different aspect of their identity or a strategy for managing change, allowing peers to explore and 'unlock' stories about acceptance, growth, and resilience in an immersive gallery walk.

s.t: Seesaw App Smash (Digital Wellbeing Journals + ChatterPix Kids)
s.d: Students maintain a digital wellbeing journal in Seesaw, reflecting on strategies for managing change and emotional responses. For key moments, they use ChatterPix Kids to animate a self-portrait or symbol, recording their voice to share personal insights or advice about navigating change, then embed these videos in their Seesaw journal.

Students collaboratively design empathy map posters in Canva, visually representing the feelings, needs, and strategies of someone experiencing change (e.g., starting at a new school, going through puberty, or facing online challenges). These can be shared digitally or printed for classroom discussion.

Students contribute to a shared Padlet wall, posting digital sticky notes with strategies, resources, or stories about managing change. They can comment on and 'like' each other's posts, creating a dynamic, student-driven resource bank that grows throughout the unit.

Each student authors a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio reflections, and video clips to document their learning about managing change, acceptance, and growth. The final product serves as a personal guide and can be shared with younger students or families.

s.t: Lego Spike Prime (Design Cycle Project: 'Change Navigator' Robot)
s.d: Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to prototype a 'Change Navigator' robot with Lego Spike Prime. The robot could be programmed to deliver supportive messages, suggest strategies, or even respond to different scenarios about change (e.g., using sensors to trigger advice for feeling nervous or excited). Teams document their process and present their robots, reflecting on how technology can support wellbeing and transitions.

plannerText: This unit explores how managing change requires acceptance, knowledge, and strategies, with a focus on relationships, digital behaviours, and personal growth. Students engage in reflective journaling, digital art, empathy mapping, and collaborative discussions, culminating in creative projects that support wellbeing and positive transitions.

ci: The media engages its audience and invites a response
lo: How media targets audiences; Techniques used to engage and persuade; Critical evaluation of media messages

s.d: Students design and build immersive 3D virtual 'advertising worlds' where visitors interact with persuasive media, ethical dilemmas, and alternative consumer models. Each group creates a virtual experience that challenges peers to think critically about advertising influence and sustainability, directly linking to the unit's focus on media engagement and audience response.

App smash: Students use Padlet to collaboratively brainstorm and map out the influences on their chosen product, then design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to gather real data on peer influences and advertising effectiveness. This workflow mirrors the unit's research and analysis tasks, making student inquiry visible and actionable.

Students compose and produce original persuasive jingles, slam poems, or rap songs that critique or promote ethical consumer choices. These audio creations are shared as part of their multimodal presentations, directly supporting the unit's emphasis on media, persuasion, and creative response.

Students create dynamic, data-driven infographics and digital posters that visually communicate the impact of advertising strategies, peer influence, and environmental consequences of consumer choices. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to remix product images, replacing the banned green screen kits.

Students become school reporters in Minecraft, investigating media sources, evaluating bias, and uncovering the truth behind advertising claims. This lesson directly supports critical thinking about media engagement and audience response, as outlined in the unit's central idea.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students prototype and build interactive 'ethical consumerism' arcade games using Makey Makey and everyday materials. Players must make choices about products, advertising, and sustainability, receiving real-time feedback on the consequences of their decisions. This hands-on project brings the unit's inquiry into media influence and responsible action to life through physical design and iteration.

plannerText: Students collaboratively investigate how media and advertising influence consumer choices, conduct surveys and interviews to analyse peer and social influences, and research the ethical and environmental impacts of products. They present findings through multimodal texts (including songs, advertisements, and slam poems) and take action by creating persuasive media or advocating for more ethical consumer practices.

ci: An inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities; the structure and function of organisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students create 3D models of interconnected human-made systems?like supply chains or transportation networks?on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate the flow of goods, information, and influence. This app smash brings abstract systems thinking to life, allowing learners to manipulate variables and observe ripple effects in real time. The activity supports inquiry into how advertising, peer influence, and ethical choices shape communities and consumer behaviour.

Groups create and distribute digital surveys to the school community to gather authentic data on peer influences, advertising effectiveness, and ethical considerations in consumer choices. Students then analyse the results in Excel to identify trends and present their findings as part of their inquiry into how organisations shape consumer behaviour.

Students collaboratively produce persuasive multimodal advertisements or slam poems for alternative, sustainable products using Canva's video and design tools. They leverage the 'Remove Background' feature for dynamic visuals and integrate real data to manipulate audience perception, directly linking to the unit's focus on advertising and influence.

Classes use Padlet to crowdsource and visually map the production, marketing, and disposal journeys of selected products. Each group posts images, research, and ethical/environmental findings, allowing for a collaborative, interactive exploration of the structure and function of organisations within global systems.

Students compose and record original audio advertisements, raps, or slam poems critiquing consumerism and proposing sustainable alternatives. They incorporate sound effects and persuasive language, then share their creations with the school community to spark discussion about responsible action and interconnected systems.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and program a 'sustainable supply chain robot' with Lego Spike Prime. The robot simulates moving products through different stages (production, marketing, disposal), and students iterate their designs to minimise environmental impact, reflecting on how human-made systems can be reimagined for a fairer future.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 6 students in an inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities, focusing on how advertising, peer influence, and ethical considerations shape consumer choices. Students collaboratively research, analyse, and present findings on selected products, create persuasive multimodal texts, and explore alternative models of consumerism that honour fairness and sustainability.

ci: Scientists undertake experimentation to confirm, contest and create new theories.
lo: The scientific method; How experiments lead to new understandings; The relationship between planets and the Sun; Energy transfer and transformation in circuits

s.d: Students use Delightex to collaboratively design and simulate 3D models of scientific experiments related to energy transfer, chemical changes, or planetary movement. They can virtually test hypotheses, manipulate variables, and visualize outcomes, then export their 3D worlds to share findings with peers, making the scientific method immersive and interactive.

Students create augmented reality models of electrical circuits, reversible/irreversible changes, or planetary systems using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record short video explanations overlaying their AR models, demonstrating their understanding of scientific concepts and inquiry methods.

Groups design visually compelling infographics or animated presentations in Canva to communicate the results of their science fair investigations, focusing on how experimentation confirms, contests, or creates new theories. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature for images of their experiments and embed data visualizations.

Students design and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect data on classmates' hypotheses, experiment results, or scientific misconceptions. They analyze the data in Excel, drawing evidence-based conclusions and reflecting on the importance of data in scientific inquiry.

Students engage with the 'Minecraft: The Scientific Method' lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-scientific-method), where they plan and conduct experiments in a virtual world, practicing variables, fair testing, and data collection, directly linking to the unit's focus on how scientists experiment to build knowledge.

For the Design Cycle STEM project, students use Lego Spike Prime to design, build, and iterate a working model that demonstrates energy transfer (e.g., a solar-powered vehicle or a Rube Goldberg machine). They follow the full design process: identifying a scientific question, planning, prototyping, testing, and refining their model, then presenting their findings and reflecting on how their experimentation mirrors real scientific inquiry.
