Students explore 'The Hall of Inventions' in Minecraft Education, then design and build their own innovative marketplace inventions in the virtual world, reflecting on how goods and services have evolved and are exchanged over time.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and build interactive, physical 'point of sale' systems for their classroom businesses with Makey Makey and everyday materials. They design, test, and refine their devices to simulate real-world transactions, integrating digital and physical elements for a STEM-rich marketplace experience.

plannerText: Students investigate how goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace by simulating a class economy, creating their own businesses, applying for jobs, managing budgets with spreadsheets, and engaging in trading sessions. They explore concepts such as supply and demand, ethics, and consumer rights through collaborative projects, role play, and digital tools, culminating in a hands-on, student-driven marketplace experience.

ci: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society
lo: How inventions have changed over time to meet the demands of society; The UN Sustainable Development Goals and the global problems they address; Using the design thinking process to create innovations that respond to real-world needs

s.d: Students design a 3D interactive prototype of their invention using Merge Cubes, then use Canva's AI image generation to create futuristic visual overlays and AR labels. This app-smash allows students to bring their SDG-inspired inventions to life, letting peers and families explore their models in augmented reality at the Future Thinkers' Forum.

Create a collaborative digital 'Invention Timeline' Padlet where students post images and short videos of inventions they've researched, annotated with how each responded to changing societal needs. Students can comment on each other's posts, sparking discussion about innovation and transformation.

Students explore the virtual museum of key innovations, then design and build their own SDG-inspired invention in Minecraft, justifying how it addresses a current global need. This links directly to the unit's focus on inventions as responses to societal change.

Students document their design process in a multimedia digital journal using Book Creator, combining photos of prototypes, voice recordings explaining their thinking, and reflections on how their invention addresses their chosen SDG.
Students use Forms to gather feedback from peers, teachers, and families on their invention prototypes, analysing the data to refine their designs and better meet the needs of their target audience.

Using the Design Cycle, students physically prototype their SDG-inspired invention with 3D printers. They iterate based on feedback, present their models at the Future Thinkers' Forum, and reflect on how their design process mirrors real-world innovation responding to societal needs.

plannerText: Students investigate how inventions and initiatives are responses to changing societal needs, exploring past and present innovations, analysing how inventions evolve, and using the design process to create their own SDG-inspired prototypes. Activities include research, science lab experiments, collaborative design thinking, and presenting inventions at the Future Thinkers' Forum.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for conservation and environmental stewardship; Ways in which First Nations land management practices can drive positive environmental change

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of local environments or endangered habitats using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera. They record short video explanations of stewardship actions, then use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to overlay themselves as digital guides within their virtual environments. This immersive, hands-on workflow brings conservation and land management to life, allowing students to 'step into' their own digital habitats.

Set up a collaborative digital wall where students post photos, videos, and reflections from their exploration of local environments, native gardens, and stewardship activities. They can map connections between Indigenous and non-Indigenous perspectives, and crowdsource ideas for conservation action.

Students author digital books documenting their journey through the unit, integrating research on habitats, stewardship, and Indigenous land management. They can embed audio interviews, photos from excursions, and their own conservation pledges.

Students virtually step into the shoes of wildlife rangers, exploring how science and community action protect habitats and species. They learn about conservation roles and design digital solutions for real-world environmental challenges.

Students design visually impactful conservation campaign posters or infographics, using Canva's templates and 'Remove Background' tool to highlight local stewardship efforts and Indigenous perspectives. These can be shared digitally or displayed around the school to inspire action.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and 3D print models of sustainable garden beds or habitat features for the school grounds. They research native species, design for biodiversity, and present their prototypes as part of a stewardship action plan, connecting digital design with real-world environmental impact.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in the concept of stewardship, exploring both Indigenous and non-Indigenous connections to land, conservation, and environmental responsibility. Activities include mapping world biomes, writing emails to local trees, investigating land management practices, and hands-on stewardship projects in the school garden, all with a strong focus on action and student agency.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has had a lasting impact on different countries and their people
lo: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; How colonisation transformed society and culture; The responsibility of colonisers and the perspectives of those colonised

s.d: Students create interactive 3D Merge Cube experiences that allow users to explore different perspectives of colonisation?such as the viewpoint of Indigenous peoples, colonisers, and contemporary Australians. Students then app smash by embedding their Merge Cube AR videos and reflections into a multimedia Book Creator eBook, presenting a layered, immersive narrative of colonisation's impact.

Launch a collaborative digital timeline where students post research, images, and primary sources about colonisation in Australia and other countries. Each post can include student voice recordings, artwork, or short video reflections, allowing for a rich, multi-modal exploration of the central idea.

Students design persuasive infographics or digital posters that highlight the lasting impacts of colonisation on different countries and their people. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to create striking visuals that juxtapose pre- and post-colonisation imagery.

Curate a playlist of documentary clips and historical re-enactments about colonisation. Students use ClickView's interactive video features to annotate, ask questions, and respond to key moments, deepening their understanding of causation and change.

Students explore the museum of key innovations from history, then research and build their own 'inventions' that either resulted from or responded to colonisation. This activity encourages students to consider how technological and cultural changes were shaped by, and in turn shaped, colonisation.

Using the Design Cycle, students design and 3D print artefacts that represent both Indigenous cultures and colonising societies. They prototype, test, and refine objects such as tools, symbols, or monuments, then present their creations alongside a reflection on how these objects tell the story of colonisation's impact.

plannerText: Students explore the reasons, methods, and impacts of colonisation through activities such as perspective-taking with 'The Rabbits', research projects on British and global colonisation, and oral presentations. The unit emphasises empathy, critical thinking, and student agency as learners investigate historical and contemporary effects of colonisation on societies and cultures.

ci: Values, beliefs and actions contribute to a diverse society
lo: How our values and beliefs shape our identity and interactions; Our responsibility to manage emotions and be safe online and in person; The importance of resilience, respect and positive relationships in a diverse community

s.d: Students design a 3D interactive 'Values & Beliefs Cube' using Canva to create each face, representing different values, beliefs, and actions from their own lives and diverse cultures. These are then uploaded to Merge EDU, allowing students to explore each other's cubes in augmented reality, sparking discussion about diversity and empathy in a hands-on, immersive way.

Create a collaborative digital 'Beliefs Wall' where students post multimedia reflections (videos, images, audio, text) about their own values and beliefs, and respond to classmates' posts to explore the diversity within their classroom community.

Students author digital autobiographies focusing on their values, beliefs, and actions, integrating photos, voice recordings, and drawings to express their unique identities and how these contribute to a diverse society.

Facilitate a 'Global Voices' project where students interview family or community members about their beliefs and values, then upload audio or video reflections to Seesaw, creating a class archive of diverse perspectives.

Students design visually striking digital posters or infographics that showcase the importance of respect, inclusion, and understanding in a diverse society, using Adobe Express's templates and 'Remove Background' feature for creative self-portraits.

Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to prototype a 'Respect Robot' with Lego Spike Prime. The robot must perform a task that symbolically represents inclusion or empathy (e.g., delivering messages of kindness or bridging two groups), requiring students to ideate, build, test, and refine their solution while reflecting on how technology can promote positive actions in a diverse community.

plannerText: The unit 'Who We Are' explores how values, beliefs, and actions contribute to a diverse society. Students engage in lessons on empathy, respect, cyber safety, and self-management, using collaborative activities, digital reflections, and real-world scenarios to deepen their understanding of identity, relationships, and responsible citizenship.

ci: Understanding global issues and using effective communication empowers us to raise awareness and inspire action.
lo: Global issues are caused by interconnected factors that require collective solutions (Causation) ??? Presentations in the media can reflect the perspective of the author rather than the truth (Perspective) ??? We must make responsible choices when responding to and creating media (Responsibility) ??? Messages can be expressed through a range of mediums and elements can be adjusted to enhance effectiveness (Communication) ??? Design choices contribute to the effectiveness of a message (Influence)

s.d: Students create an interactive augmented reality (AR) experience using Merge Cube and CoSpaces to bring their global issue campaigns to life. Each group designs a 3D scene or simulation that communicates their chosen issue, allowing viewers to explore facts, stories, and calls to action by physically rotating the Merge Cube. This immersive storytelling approach empowers students to blend digital creativity with hands-on engagement, making their advocacy unforgettable.

Students launch their own advocacy podcasts using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), where they research global issues, script compelling episodes, and interview community members or experts to raise awareness and inspire action. They collaborate in teams to plan, record, and edit their shows, using sound design and storytelling techniques to engage listeners. This real-world communication project empowers students to become changemakers and amplifies their impact beyond the classroom.

Students design visually compelling digital media campaigns?such as infographics, social media posts, and posters?using Canva. They leverage Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create striking visuals and combine text, imagery, and data to communicate their message effectively.

Students collaboratively author interactive digital books that document their inquiry journey, research findings, and action plans. These multimedia books can include embedded videos, audio reflections, and clickable links, serving as both a portfolio and a tool for advocacy.

s.t: Minecraft: Peter Is Here Ai For Cultural Heritage
s.d: Students explore how technology can be used to protect and celebrate cultural heritage sites, drawing parallels to their own campaigns for global issues. They reflect on the importance of collaboration and digital storytelling in raising awareness and inspiring action. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/peter-is-here-ai-for-cultural-heritage

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype a kinetic installation or interactive model that visually represents their global issue and proposed solutions. For example, they might build a moving sculpture that demonstrates the impact of pollution or a robotic display that responds to audience input, combining engineering, coding, and persuasive design to inspire action.

plannerText: The Year 6 'How We Express Ourselves' unit at St Kilda focuses on empowering students to understand global issues and use effective communication to raise awareness and inspire action. Students engage in inquiry cycles, develop vision statements, create media campaigns, design logos, and present live performances, all while leveraging digital tools and collaborative research to drive meaningful advocacy.

ci: The way a country is governed impacts the lives of citizens.
lo: The key institutions and functions of Australia's democratic system of government (Function) ??? How government decisions impact citizens at federal, state and local levels (Causation) ??? The rights and responsibilities of Australian citizens and their obligations as global citizens (Equality/Citizenship)

s.d: Students design an interactive 3D Merge Cube experience using Canva to create digital overlays that represent the three levels of government, their responsibilities, and how their decisions impact citizens. By scanning the Merge Cube, students can explore pop-up facts, videos, and infographics, making abstract governance concepts tactile and memorable.

Students document a 'Day in the Life' of a citizen under different government systems by creating multimedia journals in Seesaw. They can combine voice recordings, annotated images, and short videos to reflect on how laws and policies shape daily experiences, fostering empathy and global awareness.

Students explore the stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and simulate how active citizenship and government decisions can create positive change. They can build virtual monuments or scenarios that reflect the impact of governance on society.

Collaboratively build a digital debate wall where students post arguments, counterarguments, and evidence about real-world government decisions (e.g., social media bans, new laws). This interactive platform allows for peer feedback and the development of critical thinking skills.

Create dynamic, student-led video explainers or animated infographics that break down the process of how a bill becomes law in Australia. Students can use Adobe Express to storyboard, animate, and narrate the journey from idea to legislation, making civics content engaging and accessible.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype an automated voting machine or a model of Parliament using Lego Spike Prime. They must program the model to simulate voting, tallying, or decision-making processes, then reflect on how technology can support fair and transparent governance.

plannerText: In this unit, students investigate how different systems of government function, the roles and responsibilities at each level in Australia, and how government decisions impact citizens' lives. They engage in inquiry-based activities such as mapping government structures, simulating law-making, reflecting on citizenship, and proposing their own bills, culminating in creative reflections and real-world connections.

ci: The relationships between the Earth, Moon and Sun create predictable patterns that shape life on our planet and are understood through both scientific and cultural perspectives.
lo: The movement of the Earth, Moon and Sun causes predictable patterns such as day/night, moon phases, tides and seasons (Causation) ??? Different cultures, including Indigenous knowledge systems, interpret the night sky in unique and meaningful ways (Perspective) ??? The Earth, Moon and Sun have specific structures, positions and relationships that can be represented through models and simulations (Form)

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of the Earth, Moon, and Sun using Merge Cube and the Merge Explorer app on iPads. They record short video explanations using the iPad camera, then app smash with Canva to overlay cultural stories or scientific facts about celestial patterns, making the invisible visible in augmented reality.

Students use 'Minecraft: The Scientific Method' to design and run virtual experiments that model the effects of Earth's rotation, revolution, and the Moon's phases. They collect data, make predictions, and compare their findings to both scientific and Indigenous perspectives on celestial patterns.

Students script and record science podcasts using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), explaining the relationships between the Earth, Moon, and Sun from both scientific and cultural perspectives. They interview classmates, share Indigenous stories, and use sound effects to bring astronomical concepts to life, culminating in a class podcast series that makes complex ideas accessible and engaging. This project empowers students to communicate their learning creatively and reach a wider audience.

Students author digital books that combine diagrams, time-lapse photos (taken with iPad camera), and narrative text to explain the relationships between the Earth, Moon, and Sun. They include sections comparing scientific and Indigenous knowledge, and embed student-created illustrations or animations.

Students collaborate on a Padlet wall to crowdsource observations of moon phases, sunrise/sunset times, and cultural stories from their families. This shared digital space becomes a living archive of both scientific data and diverse perspectives, supporting inquiry and reflection.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype a motorized model that demonstrates the orbits and rotations of the Earth, Moon, and Sun. They program the model to show day/night cycles, lunar phases, and seasonal changes, then present their design process and findings to the class.

plannerText: Students explore the relationships between the Earth, Moon, and Sun through hands-on models, time-lapse videos, moon diaries, and cultural stories. They investigate scientific concepts like rotation, tides, and seasons, compare Indigenous and scientific perspectives, and synthesize their learning through creative projects such as podcasts, diagrams, and choice-board activities.

ci: Peoples' actions affect the balance of nature.
lo: What the environment is comprised of and how ecosystems function (Form) ??? Our responsibility to care for and protect the natural world (Responsibility) ??? The interdependence between humans, animals and the environment (Interdependence) ??? The consequences of human actions on the balance of nature (Consequences)

s.d: Students design and code interactive 3D augmented reality habitats for endangered Australian species using Merge Cube and CoSpaces. They can simulate the impact of human actions (like pollution or conservation) on the balance of nature, allowing peers to 'walk through' and interact with their virtual ecosystems in the palm of their hand.

Students explore how bees' needs and human actions affect the balance of nature by building 'beetopias' in Minecraft Education. They apply their understanding of interdependence and environmental responsibility by designing thriving bee habitats and reflecting on the consequences of human impact.

Students create persuasive digital campaigns or infographics to raise awareness about endangered species and the effects of human actions on the environment. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create visually striking posters that inspire action and empathy.

s.t: Seesaw App Smash with iPad Camera
s.d: Students document their STEM design process and field observations (e.g., at Camp Mallana or the zoo) by capturing photos and videos with the iPad Camera, then annotating, reflecting, and sharing their learning journey on Seesaw for peer and teacher feedback.

Students create a podcast series using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com) to document their STEM design projects for endangered species and share interviews with conservation experts. They plan, script, and record episodes that highlight their research, design process, and reflections on how people's actions affect the balance of nature. This authentic audio storytelling experience gives students a platform to advocate for environmental action and connect with a real-world audience.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype a robotic device that could help monitor or protect a threatened species in their local environment (e.g., a sensor to detect invasive species or a robot to distribute food or water). They research, design, build, test, and refine their solution, documenting each stage in a digital portfolio.

plannerText: The Year 6 Sharing the Planet unit focuses on how people's actions affect the balance of nature, with students investigating endangered species, conservation technologies, and Indigenous perspectives. Key activities include a STEM design project to help threatened species, excursions to zoos and natural sites, and the use of digital tools to research, design, and communicate solutions for environmental challenges.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges, risks and opportunities.
lo: The causes for and consequences of migration (Causation) ??? The changes in societies as a result of migration (Change) ??? The impact of migration on the individual, both positive and negative (Impact) ??? Migration results in a more diverse society (Diversity)

s.d: Students create interactive 3D migration stories using Merge Cubes, where each face of the cube represents a different stage or perspective of a migration journey. These are then app smashed with Book Creator to embed videos, audio interviews, and digital artifacts, allowing students to 'hold' and explore migration narratives in their hands?making the abstract concept of migration tangible and immersive.

Students conduct and record interviews with family or community members who have experienced migration, then use Seesaw to create digital portfolios that combine audio, images, and written reflections, directly connecting personal migration stories to the unit's central idea.

Using Canva, students design visually compelling infographics or digital posters that illustrate the push and pull factors of migration, integrating real-world data and personal stories to highlight the diversity and impact of migration on Australian society.

Students collaboratively build a global migration timeline on Padlet, pinning historical events, personal stories, and key migration waves. Each post can include multimedia elements such as maps, videos, and primary source documents, fostering a shared digital inquiry space.

Students create short documentary-style videos in Adobe Express, weaving together interviews, archival footage, and their own narration to explore the causes and effects of migration, culminating in a class film festival that celebrates diverse migration journeys.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype a 'Migration Journey Simulator' with Lego Spike Prime. They design and code a robotic model that simulates the obstacles, decisions, and opportunities faced during migration (e.g., crossing borders, adapting to new environments), then present their process and learning in a digital journal.

plannerText: Students explore the causes, impacts, and personal stories of migration through interviews, storyboarding, and digital presentations. They investigate historical and contemporary migration events, conduct oral histories, and present their findings using multimedia tools, with a strong focus on empathy, diversity, and the lived experiences of migrants.

ci: Understanding ourselves, our relationships and our wellbeing empowers us to thrive.
lo: The recognisable features of safe and healthy interactions (Form) ??? The connections between emotions, behaviour, learning, interactions and goals (Connection) ??? The tools and skills to stay safe online and in person (Safety) ??? The active role we play in achieving and maintaining our wellbeing (Wellbeing) ??? The strategies to achieve and maintain positive relationships (Relationships)

s.d: Students create interactive 3D wellbeing journals using Merge Cubes and the iPad camera. They record short video reflections on strategies for managing emotions, safe online interactions, and growth mindset, then overlay these onto their own Merge Cube AR models. The result is a tactile, immersive 'cube of self' that can be explored in AR, making wellbeing and identity visible and interactive.

Students design digital infographics or posters that visually map out their personal wellbeing strategies, safe online habits, and positive relationship tips. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create dynamic, inclusive visuals, and can collaborate in real time to produce a class-wide digital wellbeing gallery.

Each student authors a multimedia digital book documenting their journey through the unit. They combine photos, audio reflections, and drawings to narrate scenarios about managing friendships, responding to challenges, and celebrating their strengths, resulting in a personalised 'Who We Are' digital portfolio.

Launch a collaborative Padlet wall where students anonymously post real-life dilemmas or questions about relationships, safety, and wellbeing. Peers respond with advice, strategies, or supportive messages, building a digital community of care and shared wisdom.

Students document their learning and self-reflections through voice notes, photos, and video journals on Seesaw. They set personal wellbeing goals, track their progress, and share strategies for inclusion and belonging, inviting feedback from teachers and classmates.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype a 'Wellbeing Bot' with Lego Spike Prime. The bot could demonstrate calming breathing patterns with lights and movement, or act out positive social interactions. Students iterate their designs based on peer feedback, linking robotics to emotional regulation and healthy relationships.

plannerText: This unit focuses on understanding safe and healthy interactions both online and in real life, strategies for managing friendships and emotions, and the importance of wellbeing and growth mindset. Students engage in activities such as cyber safety lessons, wellbeing poster creation, learner profile self-portraits, goal setting, and reflective Seesaw tasks to build self-awareness and positive relationships.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to create a personal timeline, programming the car to travel along a path marked with significant events and memories from their lives. As Indi stops at each point, students share stories and artefacts, connecting past experiences to their present selves in a playful, hands-on way.

Students take photos of special objects or family artefacts and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, recording short oral stories about why these items are important. This brings personal histories to life and supports oral storytelling as seen in the unit's focus on sharing and sequencing memories.

Each student creates a digital 'My Life Story' book, combining drawings of 'baby, little, now' with voice recordings and photos. This supports the lesson sequence on personal timelines and encourages multimodal expression of past and present.

Document the inquiry process by having students upload photos of their drawings, timelines, and special objects, then record voice reflections about what they have learned about their past and family. Families can comment, fostering home-school connections and celebrating diverse histories.

Students create stop motion animations to illustrate their personal timelines, using drawings and small objects to represent key moments from their past. They narrate each scene, visually sequencing events to show how their history shapes who they are today, and share their finished animations with the class.

Design Cycle Project: Students design and build a large floor timeline using paper and images (baby, little, now). They then program Bee-Bots to travel along the timeline, stopping at each stage to trigger a student-recorded message about that life stage. This hands-on STEM project reinforces sequencing, storytelling, and the concept of change over time.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Discovering our past helps us know more about ourselves today' by sharing family stories, creating personal timelines, comparing past and present, and reflecting on personal artefacts and memories through drawing, oral storytelling, and sequencing activities.
